clean_title: sessions: ai code review doesn't allow running multiple times?
clean_body: if you run an ai code review on your changes, and then accept/reject the resulting comments, then the button for running a review seems to become disabled. in practice, i think it's pretty common for folks to run a couple passes of review--particularly with different models--just to make sure everything looks clean. and based on the current ux, i wasn't sure how to do that?
clean_text: sessions: ai code review doesn't allow running multiple times? if you run an ai code review on your changes, and then accept/reject the resulting comments, then the button for running a review seems to become disabled. in practice, i think it's pretty common for folks to run a couple passes of review--particularly with different models--just to make sure everything looks clean. and based on the current ux, i wasn't sure how to do that?
all_comments_text: this was intentional for the current design where it's not possible to set the model used for the code review. the action should become enabled again when new changes are made (we support 1 code review per turn). the idea is that the user starts a session and at the end of the turn runs a code review (probably we should automatically run it) the user then addresses these which causes changes and the user can run the code review again... |SEP| ah i see. in practice, i'll also sometimes do multiple review passes, even with the same model. since that almost always yields additional opportunities. so i just wonder whether it'd make sense to allow arbitrary reviews? |SEP| will allow running code review multiple times for the same session version. there are cases in which no review comment is produced, these cases might be hard to tell as the button will just enable again without a message indicating that no comments were produced during the previous code review. i am updating the tooltip/hover to mention that the previous code review did not produce any comments but that the user can run the code review again

clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug when using the [inline code] operators on mps, pytorch raises an exception that they are not implemented, suggesting to use the cpu fallback with [inline code] . however, the [inline code] operators are silently broken when using the cpu fallback, as shown by this reproduction case: [code block] if you run [inline code] , the assertion will fail on every rank except 0, because [inline code] didn't actually modify [inline code] . initially, it seems like this could be fixed by adding mutable aliases to the schema for [inline code] , so [inline code] knows to copy back to the input tensors: [url] however, this does not work as the [inline code] operators can be async: 1. [inline code] has already copied from the cpu tensor back to mps by the time it returns, before the async op is complete 2. the result stored in the returned [inline code] will be the cpu tensor in fact, this does not even reliably work for [inline code] with gloo, since gloo does all operations async and relies on python to handle [inline code] : [url] here's a reproduction case for [inline code] that more clearly demonstrates what's going on: [code block] potential fix a potential fix would be to: 1. add mutable aliases to the schemas for all the [inline code] operators 2. have [inline code] handle [inline code] by wrapping it to do the copies (and return the original tensor) once the original [inline code] is complete however, this is a lot of complexity that probably isn't worth it -- more likely, [inline code] should reject the [inline code] operators (and ideally gloo could natively support mps 🙂) versions [code block] cc -huang

clean_title: type mismatch in tf.maximum with mixed int/float arguments in xla compilation mode
clean_body: issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? yes source source tensorflow version 2.20.0 custom code yes os platform and distribution linux ubuntu 24.04 mobile device no response python version 3.12 bazel version no response gcc/compiler version no response cuda/cudnn version no response gpu model and memory no response current behavior? tf.maximum function fails with type mismatch error in xla compilation mode (.function(jit compile=true)) when arguments have different types (int vs float), while the same code works in eager mode. the error message is: "input 'y' of 'maximum' op has type float32 that does not match type int32 of argument 'x'." standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]
clean_text: type mismatch in tf.maximum with mixed int/float arguments in xla compilation mode issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? yes source source tensorflow version 2.20.0 custom code yes os platform and distribution linux ubuntu 24.04 mobile device no response python version 3.12 bazel version no response gcc/compiler version no response cuda/cudnn version no response gpu model and memory no response current behavior? tf.maximum function fails with type mismatch error in xla compilation mode (.function(jit compile=true)) when arguments have different types (int vs float), while the same code works in eager mode. the error message is: "input 'y' of 'maximum' op has type float32 that does not match type int32 of argument 'x'." standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]
all_comments_text: the issue appears to be caused by mixed dtypes passed to tf.maximum in xla mode. python literals (0, 1) are inferred as int32, while x is float32, which xla does not allow. explicitly matching the dtype resolves the problem without changing behavior: tf.maximum( tf.constant(0.0, dtype=x.dtype), tf.constant(1.0, dtype=x.dtype) - tf.abs(x) ) alternatively, using tf.zeros like / tf.ones like also works. this keeps eager behavior unchanged and makes the code xla-safe. |SEP| hi -tree, thanks for reporting this. i've tried suggestion replacing the integer literals with dtype-matched constants, it's working fine in this [gist]( [url]
solution_comments: the issue appears to be caused by mixed dtypes passed to tf.maximum in xla mode. python literals (0, 1) are inferred as int32, while x is float32, which xla does not allow. explicitly matching the dtype resolves the problem without changing behavior: tf.maximum( tf.constant(0.0, dtype=x.dtype), tf.constant(1.0, dtype=x.dtype) - tf.abs(x) ) alternatively, using tf.zeros like / tf.ones like also works. this keeps eager behavior unchanged and makes the code xla-safe.

clean_title: copilot chat shows “contact your admin” for models on personal github account (vs code insiders)
clean_body: - copilot chat extension version: 0.40. [hash] - vs code version: vs code insiders 1.112.0-insider (universal) - os version: macos 15.6.1 (24g90) (apple silicon, macbook pro m4 pro) - feature (e.g. agent/edit/ask mode): copilot chat (model selector) - selected model (e.g. gpt 4.1, claude 3.7 sonnet): claude opus 4.6 / claude sonnet 4.6 / gpt-5.4 - logs: no specific error logs appear in the output panel. (it's empty.) the issue occurs in the model selector ui where certain models show "contact your admin" despite the account being a personal github account. steps to reproduce: 1. open vs code insiders. 2. open copilot chat panel. 3. click the model selector dropdown. 4. observe that several models (claude opus 4.6, claude sonnet 4.6, gpt-5.4) appear but cannot be selected. 5. the ui displays the message "contact your admin". expected behavior: models should either be selectable or clearly indicate that they are unavailable due to plan restrictions. note: i was able to use claude opus 4.6, claude sonnet 4.6, gpt-5.4. since yesterday, i am unable to use them. actual behavior: the ui displays "contact your admin" even though the account is a personal github account (not part of an organization) with github education verification and copilot pro usage available. additional context: - the github account is a personal account , not part of any organization or enterprise workspace. - the account is github education verified and has access to copilot pro features. - premium usage quota has not been reached (approx. 68% used at the time of the screenshot). - some models appear in the dropdown but are not selectable and display the message "contact your admin". - this message is confusing because the account does not have an admin or organization controlling copilot settings. question: is this expected behavior due to plan restrictions or staged model rollout , or is the ui incorrectly showing enterprise policy messaging for personal copilot users? screenshots are attached showing the model selector and copilot usage panel.

clean_title: numerical discrepancy in nn.embedding between eager and compile modes

all_comments_text: the numerical discrepancy is a benchmarking artifact caused by nn.module.to() operating in-place, which permanently truncates the shared embedding weights to float16 during the third test step before the float32 compiled mode even executes. [inline code] (in [inline code] ) defines [inline code] 2. [inline code] (same file) converts capabilities to [inline code] but drops [inline code] — it's not mapped to any field 3. [inline code] checks [inline code] first, but it's always empty for byok models, so it falls through to hardcoded name matching or the learning state machine expected behavior setting [inline code] in a byok model config should cause the model to use [inline code] instead of the default [inline code] . actual behavior [inline code] is silently ignored. the edit tool is determined by: 1. hardcoded model name matching ( [inline code] — names containing 'gpt'/'openai' → applypatch, 'sonnet' → replacestring) 2. the learning state machine (defaults to replacestring for unknown models) suggested fix either: - map [inline code] through to [inline code] during byok model registration so [inline code] picks it up - or have [inline code] include [inline code] in a field that [inline code] can read from the endpoint reproduction [code block] the model will still get [inline code] / [inline code] instead of [inline code] . workaround name the model to include 'gpt' or 'openai' to trigger the hardcoded preference in [inline code] . references (in microsoft/vscode-copilot-chat) - [inline code] — [inline code] and [inline code] - [inline code] — [inline code] from [inline code] - [inline code] — [inline code] and [inline code]

clean_title: github copilot extension v0.39.0 incompatible with vs code 1.111
clean_body: does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: yes/no - na as this is issue with vs code to copilot only. after updating to the latest version of vs code (1.111), the github copilot extension v0.39.0 becomes incompatible and is disabled by the editor. vs code reports that the extension is not compatible with the current version of the editor even though both components are the latest available versions at the time of writing. this prevents using copilot functionality such as inline suggestions and copilot chat in the editor. - copilot chat extension version: 0.39.0 - vs code version: 1.111.0 - os version: windows nt x64 10.0.26100 steps to reproduce: 1. install or update to vs code 1.111 2. install github copilot extension v0.39.0 3. restart vs code if prompted 4. open the extensions view 2. it doesn't anymore now, with message invalid extension detected expected behavior github copilot extension should load normally and provide inline suggestions and copilot chat functionality. actual behavior vs code shows that the extension is incompatible with the current version and disables it automatically. as a result: copilot suggestions are not available copilot chat cannot be used extension appears disabled due to incompatibility additional context this issue appears to occur immediately after updating vs code to version 1.111 with copilot extension version 0.39.0 installed.

all_comments_text: cc |SEP| how i reproduce this locally: [code block] |SEP| for [inline code] , [inline code] and [inline code] models: imo, comparing regressions using absolute binary equal operations is too strict. it does not allow for small / non-meaningful numerical differences. a single pixel in the mask that changes from (using a threshold of 0.5 as an example) 0.4999999 to 0.500001 would trigger the whole test to fail. even if computationally those are equivalent and just small numerical discrepancies have been introduced. it is practice the standard to use similarity metrics in those cases. the same as we do with numerical comparisons for other models. so, imo all accuracy tests for masks should be using similarity metrics and not absolute equal comparisons. and looking at nightlies is a half-story. the problem is that this test have been failing consistently for a long while. and this can be observed into this signal flakiness, where about 10% of the time we got errors that [inline code] was failing. |SEP| for [inline code] , the change is from the reversion of addmm fusions (see [url] since this was already a reversion due to numeric instability, i am not sure what the right action should be, but cc'ing as well since they were the original pr author |SEP| for the illegal memory access error, this is fixed by building pytorch from main to include [url] so, the failure is the same as [url] are we sure that we have cherry pick all the change we need there into 2.10? it turns out that the cherry pick was from yesterday and hasn't gotten into rc yet, i'll test it again once rc5 is out [code block] |SEP| i have been able to get to the bottom of [inline code] issue. it turns out that [url] is a red herring. the real root cause is that benchmarking [inline code] with 2.10 rc needs [inline code] and [inline code] built from source and and both of them are 3-year old [url] (yuck). that's the reason why [inline code] has been failing in ci since forever. on the other hand, when benchmark [inline code] on 2.9.1. i can pip install the whole list [url] and get the the latest version of torchrec [inline code] fbgemm-gpu [inline code] torchrec [inline code] fbgemm-gpu pinned commits to fix this. but we can cross this off the list now because this is not a real regression |SEP| also, for sam's fail accuracy, i can confirm now that it's flaky, and also not a real regression. we can cherry pick [url] to get release ci to green. thank you jean for the timing fix! |SEP| hi and looks like sam error is still happening on main: [url] |SEP| thanks for the headsup, i'll try to reproduce this locally and find a more suitable threshold for sam, maybe, in this case, let me revert the [pr]( [url] as it should return the model accuracy to be disabled and improve on it. |SEP| confirm issue 1 is resolved: [code block] |SEP| for [inline code] i have following error: [code block] same issue with dev20251121+cu128. trying to repro on 2.9.1: [code block] |SEP| confirmed failure on 2.8.0 as well: [code block] |SEP| ty for confirm that [inline code] isn't a new regression! given that it has never been working correctly for a long time, let's skip it [url] |SEP| and here is the pr to bump torchrec and fbgemm pinned commits [url] under testing |SEP| for [inline code] , i will verify it again with the final rc next week, i couldn't reproduce it locally anymore with the current rc5 |SEP|

all_comments_text: and i also had feedback on local sessions for the sessions app today: - we were doing various local agentic work in insiders - we move over to sessions and would like to pick up that work from insiders - it felt unexpected that there isn't a way to view our local agent work (though i understand it's an expected state at the moment, given the cli-focused path in sessions) i wonder if there's anything else we might be able to do here to 1) make users aware that this is expected, 2) potentially help them still maneuver to local session in the meantime. some ideas i had (these likely aren't the best, but i figured i'd share in case there could be any useful user insight from them): - keep local sessions in a "hidden" state that's initially filtered out, but users could adjust their filters to open local sessions. and if it truly isn't feasible to open / interact with such requests from sessions, then potentially guiding users to open insiders to interact with them. this way, it feels like sessions is still fully aware of all of my agentic work from insiders (a lot of which is the local agent), and it can help me get back to that local work when i want. - if we choose to surface local sessions in history, could we automatically guide users to "continue in" the copilot cli if they try to open or send a message to a local session? that way they still interact with copilot cli in sessions, but they can also continue local agent work related: would it be helpful to file an issue regarding what the "local" agent story for sessions would look like? as i write out the potential ideas above, they probably feel more like workarounds rather than the true solutions to our local agent story. |SEP| there is no future for "local" chat sessions in vs code, i.e. they will never be surfaced in the sessions app and will very likely have to change in vs code going forward. i.e., vs code will have to adopt a new runtime that is working on where you pick "copilot cli" or "vs code cli" or "other vendor cli" agent loop and then it will appear in the sessions app too.

all_comments_text: i don't think it's a race condition, or even specific to metal, i.e. cuda (or any other gpu) has similar limit, although it's larger than one for mps |SEP| i'm not 100% sure. at the risk of being too verbose, the way i wrote [inline code] is: one thread is created for each element of the input matrix $a$ (really it can be a batch of matrices, but i'll focus on the single matrix case). then for each iteration in [inline code] , each thread does the following steps: 1. calculate one element of $h i$, which is a matrix of the same size as $a$ which depends only on the values of $a$ and $i$. each thread writes the element to a buffer. this buffer is reused on each iteration. 2. [inline code] 3. calculate one element of a matmul $h {0...i} = h {0...i-1} h i$, where $h {0...i-1}$ is the result from the previous iteration. 4. [inline code] 5. write the calculated element of $h {0...i}$ to the result buffer. the barrier at step 2 is necessary to make sure all elements of $h i$ are written to memory before the matmul is calculated by any of the threads. barrier at step 4 is to make sure all threads wait until the whole matmul is calculated before any threads go ahead to calculate the next $h i$ and overwrite the values in the existing buffer for it before all threads got a chance to read the previous value out. if $a$ (and thus $h i$) has more elements in it than the number of threads in a threadgroup, then to me it seems like the barrier won't properly sync all the threads the way the algorithm requires. or is there something else i'm missing now? if there really is a race, then i think the solution is probably to make only one thread group operate on $a$, and each thread can calculate multiple elements if the number of elements is greater than the maximum thread group size. but of course if $a$ is batched, we can create a separate thread group for each batch. let me know what you think |SEP| i think your concern is valid, and the ambiguity here is exactly where correctness bugs tend to hide. the key question seems to be the synchronization scope, not metal vs cuda per se. [inline code] only provides ordering within a single threadgroup. if [inline code] (and thus [inline code] ) spans more elements than can be covered by one threadgroup, then multiple threadgroups will necessarily participate in producing or consuming [inline code] . in that case, the barriers at steps (2) and (4) cannot guarantee the global ordering that the algorithm assumes. in other words, the algorithm requires: 1. all elements of [inline code] to be fully materialized before any thread computes [inline code] 2. all elements of [inline code] to be fully consumed before any thread overwrites the buffer for [inline code] these are global synchronization requirements. a threadgroup-level barrier is insufficient unless we can prove that a single threadgroup owns the entire matrix (or batch slice). i agree this is not unique to metal — cuda has the same limitation — but cuda implementations typically avoid this pattern by either: 1. enforcing one block per matrix (with each thread handling multiple elements), or 2. introducing kernel boundaries between iterations to obtain a global sync. so if the current mps implementation allows multiple threadgroups to participate in a single [inline code] , then this is very likely a real race, not just a theoretical one. the fact that it may “usually work” would be consistent with a latent synchronization bug. your proposed fix (one threadgroup per matrix, with striding inside the group) seems like the safest correctness-preserving approach, especially for non-batched or small-batch cases. kernel-splitting would also be a correctness-first alternative if performance allows. happy to be corrected if there's a guarantee that [inline code] never crosses a threadgroup boundary — but if not, i think this deserves a fix or clean_title: torch.compile fails with notimplementederror for mkldnn layout tensor: 'aten::addmm.out' not available for mkldnncpu backend
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug summary torch.compile raises notimplementederror when executing a compiled model with mkldnn-layout tensor input, while eager mode works correctly. the error occurs because aten::addmm.out is not implemented for the mkldnncpu backend. this prevents users from combining torch.compile optimization with mkldnn tensor format, which is a common use case for cpu performance optimization. root cause analysis the issue is a backend dispatch mismatch between eager and compiled execution: eager mode: when input is mkldnn layout, nn.linear dispatches to mkldnn. linear pointwise which handles mkldnn tensors correctly. compiled mode: inductor generates code using aten::addmm.out, which does not have an implementation for the mkldnncpu backend. the compiled graph should either preserve the mkldnn-specific operator dispatch or convert mkldnn tensors to dense format before using aten::addmm.out. expected vs actual behavior expected behavior: torch.compile should work with mkldnn tensor inputs (same as eager mode), or a clear error should be raised at compile time indicating that mkldnn tensors are not supported. actual behavior: eager mode: works correctly ✓ compiled mode: raises notimplementederror at runtime ✗ fuzzer compliance checklist this bug was discovered by fuzzing. topic: fuzzer (label request) rtol/atol: n/a (runtime crash, not numerical comparison). fp64 ref: n/a (execution crashes before output). fallback random=true: enabled in reproducer. rng seed reset: enabled in reproducer. minimal reproducer [code block] error logs === configuration === x dense: shape=(3, 10), device=cpu, is mkldnn=false x mkldnn: shape=(3, 10), device=cpu, is mkldnn=true --- dense tensor: eager --- eager out: shape=(3, 5), device=cpu, is mkldnn=false --- dense tensor: torch.compile --- compiled out: shape=(3, 5), device=cpu, is mkldnn=false values match: true --- mkldnn tensor: eager --- eager out: shape=(3, 5), device=cpu, is clean_title: open terminal output as an editable text buffer
clean_body: summary add a built-in option in the vs code terminal to quickly open the current terminal output in a new editable text document within the editor. description when running programs in the integrated terminal, the output often contains logs, stack traces, or other information that developers want to inspect, search, or edit more efficiently. while the terminal supports basic search, it lacks many powerful editing and navigation features available in regular editor files. this feature would provide a quick way to convert the current terminal buffer into a normal text document inside the editor. proposed solution add an action that allows users to copy the current terminal content into a new editor tab as plain text. possible access points: - a button in the terminal ui - a command in the command palette , e.g. [inline code] - optional right-click context menu entry in the terminal when triggered: 1. the current visible or buffered terminal output is captured. 2. a new untitled text file is opened in the editor. 3. the terminal content is pasted into that file. benefits this would allow developers to immediately use full editor capabilities, including: - advanced search and replace - multi-cursor editing - code folding - better navigation - saving logs for later inspection - sharing formatted output with others example use cases - inspecting long build logs - analyzing test failures - editing copied stack traces - extracting useful parts of cli output - saving runtime logs for documentation or debugging why this matters the vs code editor provides a significantly richer text manipulation experience than the terminal view. bridging the gap between the terminal output and the editor would improve developer productivity and reduce friction when working with large or complex terminal logs. optional enhancements - option to include full terminal scrollback vs visible output only - automatically assign a language mode like [inline code] or [inline code] - option to auto-save as a log file --- in short: a simple "open terminal output in editor" action would make it much easier to analyze and work with terminal output using the full power of the vs code editor.

all_comments_text: i was able to reproduce your issue on colab using tensorflow version 2.19.0 and 2.20.0 here, i am providing [gist]( [url] for your reference. thank you! |SEP| hi , sorry for the delay, and thanks for your patience. this happens because [inline code] internally uses the [inline code] op, which produces string tensors. xla does not support string operations, so there is no xla kernel for [inline code] . since everything inside a [inline code] function must be xla-compatible, the compilation fails. as a workaround, you can move [inline code] outside the compiled function or disable [inline code] while debugging. i tested this locally and it works fine. i have attached a colab [gist]( [url] for reference. thank you!
solution_comments: hi , sorry for the delay, and thanks for your patience. this happens because [inline code] internally uses the [inline code] op, which produces string tensors. xla does not support string operations, so there is no xla kernel for [inline code] . since everything inside a [inline code] function must be xla-compatible, the compilation fails. as a workaround, you can move [inline code] outside the compiled function or disable [inline code] while debugging. i tested this locally and it works fine. i have attached a colab [gist]( [url] for reference. thank you!

all_comments_text: additional technical detail: nvidia rtx 50xx gpus use the new blackwell architecture with compute capability sm 120. it seems that current pytorch binaries are not compiled with support for sm 120, which results in: "cuda error: no kernel image is available for execution on the device" please confirm whether sm 120 support is planned for upcoming pytorch releases, and if so, provide an estimated timeline. |SEP| additional technical details: nvidia rtx 50xx gpus use the new blackwell architecture with compute capability sm 120. it appears that current pytorch binaries are not compiled with support for sm 120, which causes all cuda kernels to fail at runtime. even the simplest cuda operation (e.g., torch.zeros(1).cuda()) results in: "cuda error: no kernel image is available for execution on the device" this strongly suggests that pytorch wheels do not yet include ptx or sass kernels targeting compute capability sm 120. could you please confirm whether official support for sm 120 (blackwell) is planned for upcoming pytorch releases, and if possible, provide an estimated timeline? many users with rtx 50xx gpus are currently unable to run any cuda workloads in pytorch due to this missing architecture support. |SEP| note that you need to install a pytorch + cuda 12.8 wheel to get blackwell support. the 12.6 wheel does not have blackwell support. |SEP| hi -byte please take a look at the following support matrix: [url] for blackwell architectures please use 12.8 or 13.0 builds. |SEP| please use [url] to ask generic questions on how to install pytorch or follow the issues reporting template ( you did not share the information what version of pytorch you've installed nor how you did it ( [inline code] as mentioned in description installs only cpu binaries on windows)

clean_title: why does register replacement not support fuzzy matching for optional parameters in operators when using search fn or search fn pattern?
clean_body: i am attempting to write a pattern matcher to match fused operators, and currently encounter an issue: neither search fn nor search fn pattern provides a way to perform fuzzy matching for optional parameters. for example, optional parameters like offset and bias in the following operator prototype appear differently in the fx graph when they are passed or omitted: operator prototype as follows: [code block] case 1 (with optional parameters): torch.ops.npu.npu quant matmul.default(arg2 1, arg3 1, arg4 1, offset=arg5 1, bias=arg6 1); case 2 (without optional parameters): torch.ops.npu.npu quant matmul.default(arg2 1, arg3 1, arg4 1); attempt 1: using search fn the following search fn only matches case 1 and fails to match case 2 (where optional parameters are omitted). to cover case 2, i have to write a separate search fn: [code block] attempt 2: using search fn pattern (callfunction) i also tried using search fn pattern (from torch. inductor.pattern matcher import register replacement as register) and wrote a callfunction as shown below. however, using ignored() results in a placeholder , which causes a failure during the matching process when verifying len(self.args)!=len(node.args). it seems impossible to match both cases with a single callfunction: [code block] impact: some operators may have more optional parameters (e.g., n optional parameters). in such cases, i would need to write 2ⁿ search fn or search fn pattern to cover all scenarios, which is extremely inefficient and unscalable. questions: 1. is there any syntax to perform fuzzy matching for this scenario, so that both cases (with/without optional parameters passed) can be matched with a single pattern? 2. what is the difference between the two writing methods in the matching process: 1.callfunction(offset=keywordarg('offset')) 2.callfunction(keywordarg('offset'))? cc -chen -nrv

all_comments_text: hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for the report. the issue occurs because the input to [inline code] is extremely large: [code block] this matrix contains over 2.1 billion float32 elements, requiring about 8.6 gb just for the input tensor. gpu-based svd needs several additional temporary buffers, so the total memory requirement can exceed 30–50 gb. such matrix sizes exceed both gpu memory capacity and the internal indexing limits of the tiling kernel (swapdimension1and2intensor3usingtiles). as a result, the kernel computes invalid tile offsets and performs out-of-bounds memory reads, which is why compute-sanitizer reports an error. when a reasonable matrix size is used (e.g., 512×512, 1024×1024, etc.), the svd op works correctly. tensorflow should ideally raise a clear resourceexhaustederror instead of allowing the kernel to proceed with invalid dimensions, so your report highlights a missing validation check. i am attaching a colab [gist]( [url] with a smaller, valid example for your reference. hope this clarifies the issue. thank you!

clean_title: proposal: explicit protocol version compatibility check in torch.distributed.init process group
clean_body: hi all, while reviewing the distributed initialization path, i was thinking about cross-version cluster scenarios (for example, mixed pytorch versions across ranks during rolling upgrades or partially upgraded environments). specifically: rank 0 running pytorch version x another rank running pytorch version y potential subtle differences in collective, rpc, or initialization semantics it seems possible that mismatched protocol expectations could result in: silent hangs unexpected collective errors hard-to-debug runtime mismatches i may be misunderstanding existing guarantees here, so i wanted to ask: 1. is there already an explicit protocol compatibility mechanism between distributed ranks during init process group()? 2. if not, would it be reasonable to introduce a minimal protocol version constant exchanged during initialization in order to fail fast on incompatible revisions? the goal would not necessarily be strict version equality, but rather to: provide a clear error when incompatible protocol revisions are detected avoid subtle cross-version failure modes before experimenting with an implementation, i wanted to get feedback from maintainers on: whether this scenario is already handled elsewhere whether such a guard would align with distributed design goals thanks! cc -huang
clean_text: proposal: explicit protocol version compatibility check in torch.distributed.init process group hi all, while reviewing the distributed initialization path, i was thinking about cross-version cluster scenarios (for example, mixed pytorch versions across ranks during rolling upgrades or partially upgraded environments). specifically: rank 0 running pytorch version x another rank running pytorch version y potential subtle differences in collective, rpc, or initialization semantics it seems possible that mismatched protocol expectations could result in: silent hangs unexpected collective errors hard-to-debug runtime mismatches i may be misunderstanding existing guarantees here, so i wanted to ask: 1. is there already an explicit protocol compatibility mechanism between distributed ranks during init process group()? 2. if not, would it be reasonable to introduce a minimal protocol version constant exchanged during initialization in order to fail fast on incompatible revisions? the goal would not necessarily be strict version equality, but rather to: provide a clear error when incompatible protocol revisions are detected avoid subtle cross-version failure modes before experimenting with an implementation, i wanted to get feedback from maintainers on: whether this scenario is already handled elsewhere whether such a guard would align with distributed design goals thanks! cc -huang
all_comments_text: this feels like a [url] topic. imo what is missing in your proposal, is why such thing is needed. this is very technically challenging problem(and significantly impact teams ability to innovate), and i can't imagine a real world scenario where something like that would be necessary/beneficial. if one plans to spawn a distributed training, they should be able to install the same version of software on all node, shouldn't they? |SEP| thanks for the thoughtful feedback, — that makes sense. you're absolutely right that homogeneous deployments are the expected and recommended model. the intent here is not to support long-term mixed-version clusters. the motivation is more about failure diagnostics than compatibility. in practice, accidental version skew can happen during: rolling upgrades in long-running clusters ci or staging environments where cached environments differ across nodes shared research clusters where jobs inherit different module environments in those cases, the resulting failure mode is often: silent hang in collective ops low-level nccl/gloo errors mysterious mismatches that are difficult to trace back to version differences the proposal is not to enable version interoperability, but simply to detect incompatible protocol revisions early during init process group() and produce a clear error message. if the position is that mixed-version deployments are unsupported and we are comfortable with current failure modes, that's completely reasonable. i mainly wanted to clarify whether: this scenario is already guarded implicitly somewhere, or there would be interest in improving the diagnostic experience for accidental skew. if this discussion would be better suited for dev-discuss, i'm happy to move it there. |SEP| i think the issue is that there would be a high likelihood that if someone ever made a change that affected the 'protocol' (which is a nebulous concept since there are multiple comms layers with their own protocols), they would probably not know to update the 'version' tag. i don't think it would be all that useful to just have one version tag per pytorch release either. also, we'd need to include things like nccl version or gloo version, which are underlying libraries used by pytorch. so i think this is probably not too actionable. |SEP| thanks and for the thoughtful feedback — this is very helpful. the maintenance burden and protocol boundary concerns make sense. if version tracking isn't consistently updated or clearly scoped across communication layers, it could create misleading guarantees rather than improve reliability. given that, and the expectation of homogeneous deployments, i agree this is probably not actionable in its current form. appreciate you both taking the time to discuss this.
solution_comments: i think the issue is that there would be a high likelihood that if someone ever made a change that affected the 'protocol' (which is a nebulous concept since there are multiple comms layers with their own protocols), they would probably not know to update the 'version' tag. i don't think it would be all that useful to just have one version tag per pytorch release either. also, we'd need to include things like nccl version or gloo version, which are underlying libraries used by pytorch. so i think this is probably not too actionable.

clean_title: missing sha256 hashes for certain artifacts on index
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug hi 👋 , the same issue was reported and fixed a few days ago in [url] for cpu builds of version = 2.9.1 but i noticed it's also the case for xpu builds and a few more are missing as well. i think the following script should list most of the affected urls that don't have a sha256 checksum: bash for index in cu126 cu128 cu130 xpu rocm7.1 rocm7.0 rocm6.4 ; do echo "index $index:" ; curl -s " [url] \ | grep -op ' = 2.9.1 cc
clean_text: missing sha256 hashes for certain artifacts on index 🐛 describe the bug hi 👋 , the same issue was reported and fixed a few days ago in [url] for cpu builds of version = 2.9.1 but i noticed it's also the case for xpu builds and a few more are missing as well. i think the following script should list most of the affected urls that don't have a sha256 checksum: bash for index in cu126 cu128 cu130 xpu rocm7.1 rocm7.0 rocm6.4 ; do echo "index $index:" ; curl -s " [url] \ | grep -op ' = 2.9.1 cc
all_comments_text: let's allocate a bit of time on monday to discuss why it keeps happening? |SEP| root cause of these sha256 missing is in our promotion script. aws copy command: [url] even thought it includes [inline code] its failing to copy the metadata in some cases. i am working on a fix currently to add sha256 recompute step after the file promotion |SEP| created [url] to address these issues. |SEP| imo it would be good to do 3 things: - add line item to the release runbook that validates all published binaries have checksum (for example by running something as simple as [inline code] which immediately identifies 21 wheels that do not have one right now) - if [inline code] is not copying metadata despite being requested to do so, we should file a support request with aws - run [inline code] from [url] which will attach the checksum to all wheels published in the release folder) |SEP| creating an issue for amazon. as per [url] should be working : --metadata-directive (string) also tried [inline code] specifies whether the metadata is copied from the source object or replaced with metadata that’s provided in the request. when copying an object, you can preserve all metadata (the default) or specify new metadata. if this header isn’t specified, copy is the default behavior. |SEP| updated metadata for torch, torchvision and torchaudio. result of the script: for index in cu126 cu128 cu130 xpu rocm7.1 rocm7.0 rocm6.4 ; do echo "index $index:" ; curl -s " [url] \ | grep -op ' |SEP| thank you for fixing it 🥇
solution_comments: root cause of these sha256 missing is in our promotion script. aws copy command: [url] even thought it includes [inline code] its failing to copy the metadata in some cases. i am working on a fix currently to add sha256 recompute step after the file promotion

[local] windows 10 vscode 1.111.0 (installed on mar 12th) [ssh remote] ubuntu 20.04 |SEP| currently only manual download + gunzip + install目前仅支持手动下载 + gunzip + 安装 i can't resolve this by gunzip, it's already a zip package for me my case is different from yours. the marketplace api returns the vspackage with content-encoding: gzip, but vs code's download logic does not decompress it before passing it to the zip extractor (yauzl). here's my evidence: 1. response headers confirm gzip encoding: content-type: application/vsix; api-version=7.2-preview.1 content-encoding: gzip 2. downloaded file is gzip, not zip: bash$ curl -l -o /tmp/test.vsix " [url] $ file /tmp/test.vsix /tmp/test.vsix: gzip compressed data, max speed, from fat filesystem (ms-dos, os/2, nt), original size modulo 2^32 9250244 3. node.js https module does not auto-decompress content-encoding: gzip: bash$ node -e " const https = require('https'); https.get(' [url] (res) = { console.log('content-encoding:', res.headers['content-encoding']); let chunks = []; res.on('data', c = chunks.push(c)); res.on('end', () = { const buf = buffer.concat(chunks); console.log('first bytes (hex):', buf.slice(0,4).tostring('hex')); // zip starts with 504b0304, gzip starts with 1f8b }); });" [code block] content-encoding: gzip first bytes (hex): 1f8b0800 4. after manual gunzip, the file becomes a valid zip/vsix: bash$ mv /tmp/test.vsix /tmp/test.vsix.gz && gunzip /tmp/test.vsix.gz $ file /tmp/test.vsix /tmp/test.vsix: zip archive data $ unzip -t /tmp/test.vsix ok so the root cause is: the marketplace returns gzip-compressed body with proper content-encoding: gzip header, but vs code writes the raw gzip bytes to disk without decompressing, then yauzl fails because it expects a zip file. this may be region-dependent — i'm in china (beijing), which might explain why you receive uncompressed zip while i receive gzip-encoded responses. environment: vs code 1.111.0 (universal), macos arm64 network: standard connection with clash tun proxy (also reproduced without proxy) |SEP| is this the same problem? |SEP| receiving same error in us, so not region-specific. some extensions install, others do not. ones that fail are gziped and fail on unzip. spent most of the day on this. frustrating. |SEP| same as |SEP| the issue has resolved on my end. i'm not entirely sure what changed — it could be a server-side fix from microsoft (the marketplace api may have stopped returning gzip-encoded responses for my region), or something changed in my network environment. for the record, the root cause was: the marketplace api returned vspackage with content-encoding: gzip, but the response body was not decompressed before being passed to yauzl for zip extraction. this may have been a transient server-side behavior. closing this issue. if anyone encounters the same problem, the workaround is to manually download the vsix with curl --compressed, which forces gzip decompression.

clean_body: issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? no source source tensorflow version tf 2.20 custom code yes os platform and distribution ubuntu 22.04 mobile device no response python version 3.10 bazel version no response gcc/compiler version no response cuda/cudnn version cuda 12.5.1, cudnn 9.2.1 gpu model and memory no response current behavior? compute-sanitizer reports an out of bounds read on deviceradixsortsingletilekernel standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]
clean_text: [cuda] illegal memory read on sparseconcat issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? no source source tensorflow version tf 2.20 custom code yes os platform and distribution ubuntu 22.04 mobile device no response python version 3.10 bazel version no response gcc/compiler version no response cuda/cudnn version cuda 12.5.1, cudnn 9.2.1 gpu model and memory no response current behavior? compute-sanitizer reports an out of bounds read on deviceradixsortsingletilekernel standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]
all_comments_text: hi , apologies for the delay, and thank you for reporting this issue. the problem is caused by invalid sparsetensor inputs, rather than an issue with the sparseconcat op itself. in your code, i noticed the following: - the indices contain extremely large negative values (- [hash] ), but sparsetensor indices must always be non-negative and within the bounds of the corresponding dense shape. - you specified shapes [0, 0, 0] for both tensors, which means the dense tensors have zero volume. a sparsetensor with shape (0, 0, 0) cannot contain any entries, yet each of your tensors has 3 values. this makes the indices, values, and shapes inconsistent. because these inputs violate sparsetensor invariants, the internal cub sort kernel receives invalid data and attempts to read from invalid memory, which triggers the out-of-bounds read reported by compute-sanitizer. when valid indices, values, and shapes are used, sparseconcat works correctly. i am attaching a colab [gist]( [url] with a valid example for your reference. i hope this clarifies the issue. thank you!

all_comments_text: hello, i can reproduce a shape inconsistency with torch.isin under torch.compile. expected: torch.isin(elements, test elements) should return a tensor with the same shape as [inline code] . observed: eager returns shape [3], compiled returns a scalar shape []. minimal deterministic repro (no randomness): python import torch device = "cuda" also repros on cpu for me elements = torch.tensor([1, 2, 3], dtype=torch.int64, device=device) test elements = torch.tensor([2], dtype=torch.int64, device=device) def func(elements, test elements): return torch.isin(elements, test elements, assume unique=false, invert=false) eager = func(elements, test elements) compiled = torch.compile(func)(elements, test elements) print("eager:", eager, eager.shape, eager.dtype, eager.device) print("compiled:", compiled, compiled.shape, compiled.dtype, compiled.device) |SEP| update from local testing: i tested the repro on macos using nightly torch 2.11.0.dev20260114. on both cpu and mps: - eager - torch.compile (default) - backend="aot eager" - backend="inductor" all return the correct shape torch.size([3]) for deterministic and issue-style (randint + .to(device)) repros. so i can’t reproduce the shape mismatch on cpu or mps. the issue is likely specific to cuda/xpu paths (or requires device-specific lowering / codegen). |SEP| fixed by [url]
solution_comments: update from local testing: i tested the repro on macos using nightly torch 2.11.0.dev20260114. on both cpu and mps: - eager - torch.compile (default) - backend="aot eager" - backend="inductor" all return the correct shape torch.size([3]) for deterministic and issue-style (randint + .to(device)) repros. so i can’t reproduce the shape mismatch on cpu or mps. the issue is likely specific to cuda/xpu paths (or requires device-specific lowering / codegen). |SEP| fixed by [url]

all_comments_text: this has the classic symptoms of a low-level driver or cudnn incompatibility, especially given the new ampere-based gpu and the recent ubuntu 24.04 release. the silent crash points away from a python-level issue and towards a problem in the underlying c++ libraries. to help narrow this down, it would be extremely useful if the original poster could provide two key pieces of information: 1. the full output of the [inline code] command. this will show the exact nvidia driver version, which is the most critical missing piece of information. 2. the result of running the same code inside the official tensorflow docker container. this is the most important diagnostic step. if the code works in the container, the problem is confirmed to be in the local environment setup. if it also fails in the container, it points to a more serious, reproducible bug in tensorflow itself. the command to test this would be: bash docker run --rm --gpus all -it tensorflow/tensorflow:2.13.0-gpu python -c "from tensorflow.keras.models import sequential; from tensorflow.keras.layers import conv2d, input; import numpy as np; model = sequential([input(shape=(32, 32, 3)), conv2d(8, (3, 3), activation='relu'), conv2d(3, (3, 3), activation='sigmoid')]); model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='mse'); model.train on batch(np.random.rand(1, 32, 32, 3), np.random.rand(1, 32, 32, 3)); print('success!')" |SEP| hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for raising your concern here. i tried running your code on google colab using tensorflow versions 2.19.0 and 2.20.0 with gpu enabled, and the code worked fine without any crashes. i noticed that you are using an older tensorflow version (2.13.0). this issue might already be resolved in the newer releases. could you please try upgrading to the latest stable tensorflow version and check again? many gpu-related issues have been fixed in recent versions, so it’s always recommended to use the latest stable release for better performance and compatibility. i am attaching the colab [gist]( [url] for your reference. thank you!

clean_title: [onnx] gridsample nodes lose all metadata props when exporting with dynamo=true and opset = 20
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug when exporting a model with torch.onnx.export(..., dynamo=true), gridsample onnx nodes have no metadata props at all (no name scopes, class hierarchy, stack trace, etc.) when using opset = 20. the same export with opset 16-18 correctly propagates all metadata. this is a regression - the fx graph capture has full source information, but the onnx translation for aten.grid sampler → gridsample drops it for newer opsets. [code block] output [code block] expected behavior gridsample nodes should have metadata props (including pkg.torch.onnx.name scopes, pkg.torch.onnx.class hierarchy, pkg.torch.onnx.stack trace) for all supported opset versions, same as other ops like gemm. actual behavior opset 16, 17, 18: gridsample has full metadata ✅ opset 20, 22: gridsample has zero metadata props ❌ impact this breaks tooling that relies on onnx node metadata to map nodes back to pytorch module hierarchy (e.g., for profiling, debugging, visualization). additional context the report=true diagnostic output shows the fx graph has correct source annotations for grid sampler, but these are not propagated to the onnx gridsample nodes for opset = 20. versions pytorch version: 2.7.0+cu128 is debug build: false cuda used to build pytorch: 12.8 cudnn version: 90701 python version: 3.10.11 (main, may 7 2023, 19:26:31) [clang 16.0.3] cuda available: true gpu: nvidia geforce rtx 4090 os: ubuntu 22.04.4 lts (x86 64) cc

clean_title: [hop] allow torch.cond (and other hop) to allow returning alias and inplace mutation when run under [inline code] or [inline code]
clean_body: 🚀 the feature, motivation and pitch fixing this should help unblock motivations (as mentioned in ) some motivation for this: firstly, the desire to do basic data-dependent computation has come up several times, but one of the key use cases is skipping the optimizer in the case of a nan gradient in mixed precision training. secondly, figuring out making torch.cond() work with cuda graphs paves the way for adding support for torch.while loop() in cuda graphs, which may be used to do moe inside a cuda graph without dropping tokens, but that is more complicated and requires its own issue to write down those issues (they go beyond just while loop support for cuda graphs; it may also need tricky memory management for autograd). repro that should work with the fix: [code block] cc: alternatives no response additional context potential patch could look like this - [url] cc: for your thoughts cc

all_comments_text: hi, yes, this is default and expected behavior in tensorflow. those early gpu/cuda “warnings” appear before the logger (absl::initializelog()) is initialized, so tf cpp min log level doesn’t suppress them. they are harmless messages, and once tensorflow is fully initialized, the log level works normally. unfortunately, there’s no built-in way to completely hide these startup messages without redirecting stderr or modifying the tf source. they can be annoying during debugging, but they do not indicate a real error and won’t appear again during model training or normal execution. |SEP| hello, thank you for a quick reply! unfortunately, there’s no built-in way to completely hide these startup messages without redirecting stderr or modifying the tf source. so is there no way at all for me to remove them? as i am playing with parallelism, they are displayed multiple times and completely clutter the output... thanks! |SEP| hi , thanks for your pointers here. hi -dorey , apologies for the delay, and thanks for raising your concern here. the tensorflow package includes gpu/xla support, which is why you are seeing those warnings. even if your machine has no gpu, tensorflow tries to initialize gpu backends like cudnn, cublas, and cufft, resulting in these warnings. [inline code] if you want to remove these warnings and use cpu only, you can install the cpu-only package with the following command: [code block] after this, importing tensorflow will show only minimal informational logs about cpu optimizations, and all gpu-related warnings will disappear. i tried this myself, and the warnings were gone. i am attaching a screenshot for your reference. thank you!

clean_title: [mps] linalg.solve backward gives wrong gradients for both a and b
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug [inline code] forward on mps matches cpu closely, but backward produces wrong gradients for both the matrix and the right-hand side. this happens on contiguous inputs too, so it's not a stride issue. [code block] output: [code block] i tested 10 different seeds and every one is wrong. the error varies a lot — well-conditioned matrices might be off by 0.2 while near-singular ones can be off by thousands (seed=1 gives ig a=13820). cuda is not affected (tested on a100 with 50 seeds, all within fp32 tolerance). [inline code] works as a workaround since it falls back to cpu for the backward. reproduces on 2.10.0 and nightly 2.12.0.dev20260217. versions [code block] also reproduces on nightly 2.12.0.dev20260217 (same machine). cc

all_comments_text: hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for the report. the issue is caused by invalid inputs passed to sparsesegmentsum, rather than a problem with the gpu kernel itself. in your code, i noticed the following: - data has shape [0, 4], which means it contains zero rows. since there are no valid rows, sparsesegmentsum cannot read from this tensor. - indices contains the values [48, 0, 116, 0]. however, with data.shape = [0, 4], all indices are out of range, because no valid index exists. because of these invalid inputs, the gpu kernel attempts to read elements from an empty buffer, which results in the out-of-bounds read reported by compute-sanitizer. when valid data, indices, and segment ids are used, the op works correctly. i am attaching a colab [gist]( [url] with a corrected example for your reference. i hope this clarifies the issue. thank you!

all_comments_text: cc thoughts ? |SEP| me, , and have talked about this a lot and it has changed over time: 1. sdpa math originally started by respecting the regular composite casting rules. 2. then we added [inline code] ([attention.cpp l866-l883]( [url] this was to more closely reflect what is happening inside the fused kernels by upcasting to fp32. however this is not perfect since the multiplications inside the kernels are not [inline code] — rather [inline code] . the main invariant we want is that the softmax scores are in fp32, so for flex-attention we cast up before the matmul so that the output doesn't get cast down (which is the default for [inline code] in pytorch). the [inline code] impls goal is to be a faithful replication of the kernel, not necessarily the highest possible precision (this we have gone back and forth on). so like flash attention, we cast p back to input dtype ([flash fwd kernel.h l346-347]( [url] prior to doing the final matmul. precision flow comparison: | stage | flash attention | flex attention math | | q @ k^t | fp16 inputs → fp32 acc | fp32 @ fp32 → fp32 | | softmax | fp32 | fp32 | | p (attn weights) | cast to query.dtype | cast to query.dtype | | p @ v | fp16 inputs → fp32 acc | input dtype @ input dtype | | output | cast to query.dtype | query.dtype | |SEP| i think i get my bug/issue now (to be 100% confirmed still..) in these tests, we're comparing different attention backends against one another: math, sdpa, flex at fp16 but under-the-hood all these implementations, although you give them fp16 as input, they do the matmul accumulation at fp32 precision.. so they end up testing just that: is the matmul done internally at fp32 precision?

clean_title: update status bar entry receives focus automatically in screen reader mode
clean_body: problem in screen reader mode, the update status bar entry appears to receive focus automatically. this unexpectedly moves focus and can interrupt the current navigation context. expected behavior the update status bar entry should not automatically receive focus in screen reader mode. actual behavior focus is moved to (or lands on) the update status bar entry without explicit user intent. why this is a problem automatic focus changes are disruptive for assistive technology users and can cause loss of context while reading or interacting with other ui elements. repro steps 1. enable screen reader mode in vs code. 2. start vs code in a state where an update status bar entry is shown. 3. observe initial focus behavior after startup / when the update entry appears. 4. focus is automatically placed on the update status bar entry. notes - this issue is specifically about behavior in screen reader mode. - the update entry should remain available and discoverable, but focus should stay where the user left it unless explicitly moved by the user.

clean_title: [bug] cuda error invalid ptx on new rtx 5080 gpu with driver 581.29
clean_body: issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? no source binary tensorflow version tensorflow 2.20.0 and 2.16.2 custom code yes os platform and distribution ubuntu 24.04.3 lts mobile device no response python version 3.11 bazel version n/a gcc/compiler version n/a cuda/cudnn version - cuda version (from nvidia-smi): 13.0 - cudnn version (installed via pip): 8.9.7 (from package nvidia-cudnn-cu12==8.9.7.29) - cuda libraries (installed via pip): compatible with cuda 12.x gpu model and memory nvidia geforce rtx 5080 laptop gpu, 16gb current behavior? current behavior when attempting to run any tensorflow operation on the gpu that requires jit compilation (e.g., tf.matmul), the execution fails with an internalerror. the detailed log output reveals that the root cause is a cuda error invalid ptx error, which occurs during the cumoduleloaddata step. this initial failure prevents the creation of a valid cuda function handle, leading to the subsequent and final cuda error invalid handle error when tensorflow tries to launch the kernel. this behavior is consistently reproducible on a new nvidia rtx 5080 laptop gpu with the latest drivers, and occurs with both tensorflow versions 2.20.0 and 2.16.2 installed from pip. key log line: expected behavior the expected behavior is that the tf.matmul operation should execute successfully on the gpu. specifically: tensorflow should correctly generate a valid ptx kernel for the new gpu architecture. the nvidia driver should accept this ptx code and successfully jit-compile it into native sass. the operation should complete without any internalerror, cuda error invalid ptx, or cuda error invalid handle exceptions. after the initial (and expected) jit compilation delay, subsequent calls to the same operation should be fast. standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]

all_comments_text: hi , thank you for raising this issue. the root cause appears to be that the current tensorflow pip binaries do not include precompiled cuda kernels for the nvidia blackwell architecture (compute capability 12.0, used in the rtx 50 series). as a result, when tensorflow falls back to jit compilation from ptx, the generated ptx code is rejected by the cuda 13.x driver, leading to [inline code] and [inline code] errors. this is a known and ongoing issue. please refer to the following tracking [issue]( [url] for further updates and technical discussions. some of the comments there may also be helpful for your setup. thank you! |SEP| i can confirm encountering this exact issue on an nvidia geforce rtx 5080 gpu using tensorflow 2.16+. the issue seems indeed related to the lack of precompiled kernels for compute capability 12.0 (blackwell) and xla compilation failures with the new drivers. however, i managed to get a stable training loop and inference running by explicitly disabling xla devices and jit compilation. workaround that worked for me: 1. disable xla devices (must be done before importing tensorflow): [code block] 2. disable jit in keras models: when compiling your model, explicitly set [inline code] : [code block] with these settings, i am able to utilize the gpu for training without the [inline code] crash. |SEP| hi, i think that this could help: [url]
solution_comments: i can confirm encountering this exact issue on an nvidia geforce rtx 5080 gpu using tensorflow 2.16+. the issue seems indeed related to the lack of precompiled kernels for compute capability 12.0 (blackwell) and xla compilation failures with the new drivers. however, i managed to get a stable training loop and inference running by explicitly disabling xla devices and jit compilation. workaround that worked for me: 1. disable xla devices (must be done before importing tensorflow): [code block] 2. disable jit in keras models: when compiling your model, explicitly set [inline code] : [code block] with these settings, i am able to utilize the gpu for training without the [inline code] crash.

clean_title: how does tensorflow manage the lifecycle of an input tensor that is used within a gpu kernel?
clean_body: issue type others have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? no source source tensorflow version tf 2.5 custom code no os platform and distribution no response mobile device no response python version no response bazel version no response gcc/compiler version no response cuda/cudnn version no response gpu model and memory no response current behavior? i've noticed that in the methods [inline code] , [inline code] , and [inline code] , there are no explicit synchronization operations for the cuda stream. if an op's input tensor is not referenced by any other ops, it will be deallocated after the op finishes. in this case, if the op's cuda kernel has not completed its execution, it could lead to accessing the memory of a deallocated tensor. if there is a mechanism that ensures this problem does not occur, i would appreciate it if you could point it out. thank you. standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]
clean_text: how does tensorflow manage the lifecycle of an input tensor that is used within a gpu kernel? issue type others have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? no source source tensorflow version tf 2.5 custom code no os platform and distribution no response mobile device no response python version no response bazel version no response gcc/compiler version no response cuda/cudnn version no response gpu model and memory no response current behavior? i've noticed that in the methods [inline code] , [inline code] , and [inline code] , there are no explicit synchronization operations for the cuda stream. if an op's input tensor is not referenced by any other ops, it will be deallocated after the op finishes. in this case, if the op's cuda kernel has not completed its execution, it could lead to accessing the memory of a deallocated tensor. if there is a mechanism that ensures this problem does not occur, i would appreciate it if you could point it out. thank you. standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]
all_comments_text: i've noticed that in the methods executorstate::processsync, gpudevice::compute, and opkernel::compute (tensorflow v2.5), there does not appear to be explicit cuda stream synchronization. my concern is that if an operation's cuda kernel has not finished executing before the input tensor is deallocated, it could result in access to freed memory or race conditions. |SEP| hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for raising your concern here we appreciate your detailed observation. you are right that cuda kernels execute asynchronously, and premature tensor deallocation could theoretically lead to race conditions or use-after-free issues. however, tensorflow manages this safely without [inline code] calls in functions such as [inline code] , [inline code] , or [inline code] . instead, tensorflow uses an event-based deferred deallocation mechanism implemented in the gpu runtime. this design ensures that memory used by a cuda kernel is not freed until the kernel’s execution has completed. here are a few references that explain this behavior: - [tensorflow gpu event manager source (gpu event mgr.cc)]( [url] handles deferred frees and event polling. - [tensorflow gpu guide]( [url] describes asynchronous gpu memory behavior and allocator configuration. - [nvidia tensorflow user guide (pdf)]( [url] documents the tf gpu allocator=cuda malloc async option for asynchronous allocation and freeing. so even though there are no explicit synchronization calls in those executor methods, tensorflow ensures correctness through deferred-free and event-driven synchronization mechanisms in the gpu runtime. i hope this clarifies your concern. thank you!

clean_title: add official icon support for .gx and .galaxy file
clean_body: hello vs code team, i have created a new programming language that uses the file extensions .gx and .galaxy. currently, these files appear with the default text icon in vs code, which makes them look like plain text files. to improve the developer experience, i designed a simple and clean icon: a purple curved line (like a galaxy spiral) with a small yellow dot/star in the diagonal corner. this icon visually represents the language and makes .gx and .galaxy files easier to identify in the editor. i would like to request official support for this icon in vs code’s built‑in file icon themes, so that these file types are recognized and displayed with their own unique symbol. i have already tested this icon in a custom extension, and it works well. adding it officially would help other developers who use .gx and .galaxy files to have a better experience without needing to install a separate extension. thank you for considering this request!

clean_title: tensorflow lite reference kernel reducesum overflows when network type not uint8
clean_body: system information - linux ubuntu 22.04 - tensorflow installed from pip source - tensorflow version 2.19.1 - tflite runtime installed from pip source - tflite runtime version 2.14.0 standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] the example network can be created from the attached file minimal.json. the network contains a single reducesum op, the input tensor has shape and type int8[1,128]. output is [[-1]] when it should be [[127]] [minimal.json]( [url] any other info / logs i have located the bug in the tensorflow lite reference kernel, in file /tensorflow/lite/kernels/reduce.cc. in the function quantizedmeanorsum, when kernel type is kreference, the call to reference ops::quantizedmeanorsum uses type uint8 instead of template type t. this results in overflow when a network has a different type. i have included the bug fix as a git patch below. [code block]

all_comments_text: hi , apologies for the delay, thanks for reporting this issue. the issue is caused by the extremely large tensor size used as input to [inline code] . you are using tensor: [code block] contains more than 2.1 billion elements and requires about 4.3 gb of memory for the input alone. a transpose operation also needs additional temporary buffers, so the total memory requirement easily exceeds 10–15 gb, which is beyond the capacity of typical gpus. additionally, these dimensions exceed the internal indexing limits of the gpu transpose kernel (swapdimension1and2intensor3usingtiles). when the grid size becomes too large, tile indexing arithmetic overflows and the kernel computes invalid memory offsets, which leads to the out-of-bounds read reported by compute-sanitizer. when a reasonable tensor size is used (e.g., 512×512, 1024×1024, etc.), the operation works normally. tensorflow should ideally raise a clean resourceexhaustederror or shape validation error, so your report highlights a missing validation check. i am attaching a colab [gist]( [url] with a smaller valid example. hope this clarifies the issue. thank you!
solution_comments: hi , apologies for the delay, thanks for reporting this issue. the issue is caused by the extremely large tensor size used as input to [inline code] . you are using tensor: [code block] contains more than 2.1 billion elements and requires about 4.3 gb of memory for the input alone. a transpose operation also needs additional temporary buffers, so the total memory requirement easily exceeds 10–15 gb, which is beyond the capacity of typical gpus. additionally, these dimensions exceed the internal indexing limits of the gpu transpose kernel (swapdimension1and2intensor3usingtiles). when the grid size becomes too large, tile indexing arithmetic overflows and the kernel computes invalid memory offsets, which leads to the out-of-bounds read reported by compute-sanitizer. when a reasonable tensor size is used (e.g., 512×512, 1024×1024, etc.), the operation works normally. tensorflow should ideally raise a clean resourceexhaustederror or shape validation error, so your report highlights a missing validation check. i am attaching a colab [gist]( [url] with a smaller valid example. hope this clarifies the issue. thank you!

all_comments_text: confirmed this repros with inductor but not [inline code] |SEP| hi after testing, i found that when [inline code] or [inline code] (which behave the same), eager and aot eager decomp partition produce the same index output, while inductor produces different results. however, when [inline code] , the index outputs from eager, aot eager decomp partition, and the inductor backend are all identical. this seems consistent with the semantics of unstable sort. eager and inductor likely use different implementations of unstable sort. i wonder whether we actually intend to require identical index outputs between eager and inductor in the unstable case? cc: |SEP| aten.sort with stable=none or stable=false is meant to be unstable sorting, so we won't be able to guarantee that the ordering match between eager and inductor. all_comments_text: hi , apologies for the delay, and thank you for raising your issue here. the problem is caused by invalid values passed to [inline code] . for [inline code] , the [inline code] tensor must: [code block] in your code, [inline code] contains very large negative values (e.g., -21168624) and very large positive values (e.g., 32582). these indices do not correspond to any valid segment for your input, which has only 6 rows. because of these invalid values, the gpu kernel attempts to perform atomic updates on memory locations outside the valid output buffer, which leads to the out-of-bounds atomic write reported by [inline code] . when valid segment ids are used, the op works correctly. i am attaching a colab [gist]( [url] for your reference. i hope this clarifies your issue. thank you!

refactor is centered in 03 continuous colocation.ipynb. i verified that the current hdr helper cell contains duplicate apply hdr s curve and render point overlay definitions, so part of the current consolidate the duplicated hdr helper functions into one canonical rendering pipeline in 03 continuous colocation.ipynb. add a user-facing colormap mode layer with exactly these options: fire, red, from style selection so the same hdr transform can render on black or white backgrounds with the appropriate point color. refactor show hdr plot to accept the colormap mode explicitly and make it the agg data objects instead of rebuilding higher-resolution canvases. update the settings dictionaries, printed settings, widget extraction, and export cells so they include the new colormap field and no all_comments_text: i would like to work on this issue. could you please assign it to me? i am investigating why the results differ between default and channels last memory formats. |SEP| this is likely to be expected behavior since kaiming uniform and uniform generate random numbers by iterating over the underlying storage. if the memory layout differs, the same random values get mapped to different logical positions in the tensor. a workaround would be to - create the model in regular format - initialize it - then convert the model to be channels last |SEP| i have implemented the native fix for this inconsistency by ensuring initialization happens on a contiguous buffer, as discussed. i've also added a regression test (linked above) that verifies bit-wise parity across memory formats. the pr is now ready for a final review.
solution_comments: this is likely to be expected behavior since kaiming uniform and uniform generate random numbers by iterating over the underlying storage. if the memory layout differs, the same random values get mapped to different logical positions in the tensor. a workaround would be to - create the model in regular format - initialize it - then convert the model to be channels last |SEP| i have implemented the native fix for this inconsistency by ensuring initialization happens on a contiguous buffer, as discussed. i've also added a regression test (linked above) that verifies bit-wise parity across memory formats. the pr is now ready for a final review.

clean_title: [release 2.11][vllm] distributed failures - free memory on device cuda:0 (19.77/22.05 gib) on startup is less than desired gpu memory utilization (0.9, 19.84 gib)
clean_body: 24.02.2026: [url] analysis: title: pytorch 2.11 + cuda 13.0 increases baseline gpu memory usage, breaking memory-tight workloads environment: - pytorch 2.11.0+cu130 - cuda 13.0 / driver 570.133.20 - nvidia l4 (22.05 gib) - ray distributed executor - vllm (pipeline parallel test) description: after upgrading to pytorch 2.11 + cuda 13.0, the baseline gpu memory consumed before any model loading has increased compared to previous pytorch/cuda versions. on an nvidia l4 (22.05 gib total), only 19.77 gib is free after cuda context initialization, whereas previously enough memory was available for a 90% utilization target (19.84 gib). the 70 mib increase in baseline memory usage causes workloads that target gpu memory utilization=0.9 to fail with an oom-style error during device initialization, before any model weights are loaded. this occurs in a ray distributed setup (2 gpus, pp=2) running microsoft/phi-3.5-moe-instruct with dummy weights. the same configuration worked on prior pytorch + cuda versions. expected behavior: baseline cuda context memory consumption should not increase significantly between pytorch releases, or at minimum should be documented as a known change. reproduction: initialize cuda on an l4 gpu with pytorch 2.11+cu130 via ray workers and check torch.cuda.mem get info() — free memory is lower than with pytorch 2.10 or earlier. [code block] cc

clean_title: copilot chat: repeated "token expired or invalid" 401 errors requiring restart
clean_body: bug description copilot chat intermittently fails with a 401 "token expired or invalid" error. this has occurred twice within a 3-hour window. the error persists on retry — clicking "try again" returns the same 401. error details [code block] does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled? no — this is specific to the github copilot chat extension. - vs code version: latest stable (march 2026) - - os version: macos 15 (darwin 25.3.0) steps to reproduce 1. use copilot chat normally in vs code 2. 2. after some period of use (variable, within 1-3 hours), the next chat request fails with 401 "token expired or invalid" 3. 3. clicking "try again" does not resolve the error — it returns the same 401 4. 4. the only workaround is to reload vs code or sign out/sign back in to github expected behavior copilot should automatically refresh its authentication token before it expires, or gracefully re-authenticate when a 401 is received, rather than entering an unrecoverable error state. actual behavior the token silently expires and copilot does not attempt to refresh it. every subsequent request (including retries) fails with the same 401 until the user manually intervenes. additional context - this happened twice in 3 hours on the same session - - signing out and back in to github resolves the issue temporarily - - - reloading vs code ( [inline code] ) also works as a workaround - - - - the token refresh mechanism appears to be broken or not triggering reliably
