all_comments_text: hi, this issue occurs because keras.layers.lstm with default parameters uses the cudnn fast path, which is not supported by xla gpu jit. a simple workaround is to disable the cudnn path by setting recurrent dropout 0. for example: layers.lstm(32, recurrent dropout=0.1, return sequences=false) this allows the lstm to compile and run under xla on gpu while still functioning correctly. this works in my ubuntu 24.04 [issue 100872.py]( [url] |SEP| hi -jain7, thanks for your pointers and quick response. hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for raising this issue. i was able to reproduce the same behavior with tensorflow 2.19.0 and 2.20.0. as -jain7 mentioned, setting recurrent dropout 0 disables the cudnn fast path and allows the lstm to compile under xla gpu jit. i tried this workaround and it works fine for me as well. here is a minimal [gist]( [url] with the workaround for reference. thank you!
solution_comments: hi, this issue occurs because keras.layers.lstm with default parameters uses the cudnn fast path, which is not supported by xla gpu jit. a simple workaround is to disable the cudnn path by setting recurrent dropout 0. for example: layers.lstm(32, recurrent dropout=0.1, return sequences=false) this allows the lstm to compile and run under xla on gpu while still functioning correctly. this works in my ubuntu 24.04 [issue 100872.py]( [url] |SEP| hi -jain7, thanks for your pointers and quick response. hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for raising this issue. i was able to reproduce the same behavior with tensorflow 2.19.0 and 2.20.0. as -jain7 mentioned, setting recurrent dropout 0 disables the cudnn fast path and allows the lstm to compile under xla gpu jit. i tried this workaround and it works fine for me as well. here is a minimal [gist]( [url] with the workaround for reference. thank you!

all_comments_text: hi , apologies for the delay, and thank you for raising this issue. the behavior you observed is expected [inline code] and [inline code] instancenorm2d are conceptually different. according to the [official keras documentation]( [url] [inline code] normalizes activations across the batch (subtracting the batch mean and dividing by the batch standard deviation). it aggregates statistics across the batch and does not normalize each sample individually. in contrast, pytorch’s [inline code] normalizes each instance independently across spatial dimensions for each channel. that’s why in your example: [code block] the output mean remains nearly the same batchnorm aggregates batch-wide statistics, so similar samples share nearly identical normalization. to replicate [inline code] behavior in tensorflow, you can use tf.keras.layers.groupnormalization with groups=channels, which normalizes each channel independently. here is the [gist]( [url] for your reference. this achieves the same normalization behavior as pytorch’s instancenorm2d(channels). you can also refer to the [groupnormalization documentation]( [url] for more details. thank you!

all_comments_text: core issue: batch size dependency the same sample produces correct output when processed individually ( [inline code] ), but produces nan when processed as part of a batch. this violates sample independence - a sample's output should not depend on other samples in the batch. key observation: - single sample: [inline code] → correct output (max: 9.95e-01) - same sample in batch: [inline code] → nan this batch size-dependent behavior causes non-reproducible results and inconsistency between different batch configurations. the fix should ensure sample independence regardless of batch size. |SEP| same sample must yield identical results whether processed in batch or alone—this is cuda's contract, data-independent. |SEP| there is no such contract, please don't make things up. does the individual sample or result have extreme values? |SEP| i've confirmed it. extreme values do exist, but regardless, single-sample and batch processing should yield identical results. [code block] |SEP| no they shouldn't and it's documented [url] |SEP| "i think this should be reopened because there's a big gap between what the docs say and what’s actually happening. the documentation on 'batched computations' mentions that results might be 'slightly different' due to optimizations. but i’m seeing the output go from a perfectly valid number (0.995) in single-sample mode to nan in batch mode. that’s not just a 'slight difference'—it’s a total failure. if the system can calculate the sample correctly on its own, it shouldn't just break because it's part of a batch. this feels like a bug in the batching logic itself, and batching should be a speed optimization, not something that makes the results invalid. would it be possible to double-check if this is indeed the expected behavior?

clean_title: sessions: worktree-init actions can feel a bit noisy after a while
clean_body: after configuring a run action to initialize worktrees, the integrated terminal will automatically open every time you start a session for that workspace. this behavior feels nice from a visibility perspective, but it's also a bit distracting when you're simply trying to start a new task, and you aren't even thinking about running anything yet. i wonder if it would make sense to suppress the terminal from opening for worktree setup tasks, and instead, show a spinner icon in the play button, that let's the user see that a run action is running, and then optionally open it if they want to see the details. that way there's still an entrypoint for knowing an action is running, and a way to view the logs, but without it cluttering every session.

clean_title: torch.compile mode='reduce-overhead' regression: 7x slower cudagraphlaunch and new cudagraph trees.py overhead in pytorch 2.10 vs 2.9
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug [inline code] has a 40-55% throughput regression in pytorch 2.10 vs 2.9 on an h100 gpu running a 6b parameter production gpt inference engine. the root cause is 7x slower [inline code] calls and new python-level overhead in [inline code] that does not exist in 2.9. we performed a controlled isolation experiment with three configurations to separate the pytorch code regression from the cuda toolkit change, and found both pytorch 2.10's inductor code and the cuda 13.x toolkit contribute , with pytorch 2.10's code changes being the primary factor. environment - gpu : nvidia h100 80gb hbm3 (modal cloud) - pytorch versions tested : 2.9.0+cu128, 2.10.0+cu128, 2.10.0+cu130 - cuda toolkits tested : 12.8, 13.0/13.1 - os : ubuntu 24.04 (container) - python : 3.10 - workload : 6b parameter gpt model, [inline code] , 160 concurrent requests, batched inference throughput regression three-way isolation experiment (4 rounds x 500 steps, round 0 discarded, 3 scored) | configuration | med tok/s | stddev | cv | regression | | torch 2.9.0+cu128 (cuda 12.8) | 8,016 | 160 | 2.0% | baseline | | torch 2.10.0+cu128 (cuda 12.8) | 6,639 | 474 | 7.1% | -17.2% | | torch 2.10.0+cu130 (cuda 13.0) | 4,792 | 233 | 4.9% | -40.2% | the isolation experiment (same cuda 12.8, different pytorch) confirms pytorch 2.10's code is responsible for at least 17% throughput loss independent of cuda version. per-round stability | config | r0 (discard) | r1 | r2 | r3 | | torch 2.9 + cuda 12.8 | 8,122 | 7,873 | 8,189 | 7,984 | | torch 2.10 + cuda 12.8 | 7,782 | 7,129 | 6,606 | 6,182 | | torch 2.10 + cuda 13.1 | 5,313 | 5,046 | 4,743 | 4,588 | notable : both torch 2.10 configs show round-over-round degradation (declining throughput over sequential measurement windows) that torch 2.9 does not exhibit. this suggests state accumulation in the cuda graph tree management code. root cause: [inline code] + [inline code] perfetto traces captured with [inline code] show: 1. [inline code] is 7x slower per call | metric | torch 2.9 | torch 2.10 | change | | [inline code] total | 41.25 ms | 293.27 ms | +611% | | [inline code] per call | 0.84 ms | 5.99 ms | +7.1x | | launch count | 49 | 49 | same | | graph instantiations | 1 | 1 | same | all 49 launches shifted from the [0.5-1.0) ms range (96% in 2.9) to the [5.0-10.0) ms range (100% in 2.10). 2. new python overhead in [inline code] | component | torch 2.9 | torch 2.10 | change | | [inline code] total | 758 ms | 2,866 ms | +278% | | [inline code] avg/event | 0.124 ms | 0.471 ms | +280% | | [inline code] avg | 4.41 ms | 7.02 ms | +59% | 3. functions that appear in 2.10 but not in 2.9 traces | function | count | total time | | [inline code] | 97 | 667.76 ms | | [inline code] | 97 | 667.36 ms | these are entirely new overhead that did not exist in the 2.9 code path. what is not the cause - graph structure : same number of graphs (1), same launch count (49), same instantiation count (1) - gpu kernels : the actual cuda kernels (gemm, attention, triton fusions) are slightly faster in 2.10 - cuda toolkit alone : keeping cuda 12.8 while upgrading to torch 2.10 still shows 17% regression - compile mode : tried [inline code] , [inline code] , [inline code] , [inline code] — none recover performance. the [inline code] baseline remains the best strategy for both versions. reproducer the workload is a production gpt model, but the issue should be reproducible with any model using [inline code] with cuda graphs. the key characteristics: [code block] expected behavior [inline code] should take roughly the same time per call in 2.10 as in 2.9 ( 0.84ms), and the new [inline code] code paths should not add 667ms of python overhead per 50 inference steps. additional context - the round-over-round degradation pattern (throughput declining across sequential measurement rounds without container restart) in 2.10 but not 2.9 suggests the cuda graph tree management may be accumulating state or doing increasing work per replay - we have full perfetto traces for both versions available on request - our production system serves 1m+ dau and we are blocked from upgrading to 2.10 due to this regression cc

all_comments_text: why are you filing this issue against pytorch and not against nvidia? cudagraphlaunch is a direct call to nvidia apis. |SEP| hi ! thanks for filing the issue, let me take a look at it. as there are many variables at play here, would you be able to share a more specific repro and/or the traces? |SEP| i've investigated concerns about slow cudagraphlaunch() recently. your slowness is likely due to profiling overhead. basically, as soon as you subscribe to cupti activity api for cudagraphlaunch(), cudagraphlaunch() does o(n) work. meanwhile, it is expected to do o(1) work normally. see figure 2 [here]( [url] feel free to try out measuring the length of cudagraphlaunch() on the cpu side to confirm. i would expect that cudagraphlaunch() takes about the same time regardless of pytorch 2.9 vs 2.10 or cuda 12.8 vs cuda 13.1 when you don't use profiling. you can do this with ebpf, or simply just do time.time() before and after graph.replay() (note that replay() launches two extra kernels in addition to cudagraphlaunch(), but i wouldn't worry about that overhead). unfortunately, i am a bit clueless on the pytorch profiler's implementation, so it's unclear to me whether you can simply disable cupti while still getting useful metrics from the pytorch profiler. (in nsys, you can just do [inline code] , for example, to capture only nvtx ranges, but not any cuda api calls). regarding cuda graph trees, you have to give more detail if you want actual help. i don't know what cudagraph trees.py(2029) refers to without an exact commit hash. even then, what you've said is not that helpful. |SEP| there's an integration point to swap in a faster cuda graph wrapper; vllm uses it. check it out. |SEP| i checked with offline and it seems we cannot repro the issue in smaller examples. a repro would be very helpful to further debug. there's an integration point to swap in a faster cuda graph wrapper; vllm uses it. check it out. please check [url] for integration example. it achieves on par or better performance than vllm's default cudagraph. |SEP| -logic you didn't conduct a logical audit on this throughput collapse, claude code did, and as such your comment violates our contributing policy (see [url] none of the copy-paste you did even remotely explains why cudagraphlaunch would become slower, and cudagraph trees didn't have any substantive changes between 2.9 and 2.10 [url] what would be the exact commit you would refer to as introducing extra overhead? no extra pytho-side validation would have any effect on cudagraphlaunch duration. |SEP| after removing the dependency of flashinfer in our prod, the combo of torch 2.10 + python 3.12 + cuda 13.1 outperforms torch 2.9 by 4%. thank you for everybody chiming in. closing for now.

clean_body: problem after upgrading to vs code 1.111.0, copilot chat repeatedly fails with 401 "token expired or invalid" errors. this has happened multiple times in the last 3 hours. starting a new chat does not resolve it — the error persists across conversations. this is a follow-up to which was closed, but the issue continues to occur. error [code block] steps to reproduce 1. upgrade to vs code 1.111.0 2. use copilot chat normally 3. after some time, all requests fail with 401 "token expired or invalid" 4. retrying does not help — the error loops 5. only a full vs code restart temporarily resolves it, but it recurs environment [code block] no updates currently available (checked via help check for updates). additional context - no issues before upgrading to 1.111.0 last night - also experiencing a separate 400 error bug () that started at the same time — both appear to be 1.111.0 regressions - previous report: (closed)

all_comments_text: i don't believe this is the same issue as . that is a long-standing meta issue from months ago covering general "request failed" errors. this is a specific regression in 1.111.0 — i had zero 401 token errors before upgrading last night. since the upgrade, it has happened repeatedly (multiple times in 3 hours). the token refresh mechanism appears to be broken in this release specifically. additionally, i'm also experiencing a separate 400 "text content blocks must contain non-whitespace text" error () that also started immediately after the 1.111.0 upgrade. two new, distinct copilot chat failures appearing simultaneously after a version upgrade strongly suggests a regression, not a pre-existing general issue. could this please be reopened and investigated as a 1.111.0 regression rather than grouped into the catch-all meta issue?

clean_title: github copilot - add the ability to view a prompt before sending
clean_body: at some point an update was made so that prompts do not fill out your chat box when you press tab to complete one. we have prompts shared between our team and we can't expect people to run other's shared prompts without being able to read them. on top of that, many prompts are setup so that you finish it out yourself, and it's hard to do that if you can't read the prompt it would be ideal if it was an option. please add the ability to turn off anything you add, as this is a terrible feature for any reasonable developer. prompts are auto-loaded from mcps and extensions, this is dangerous really. cursor does this too, and it's not usable as a developer tool.
clean_text: github copilot - add the ability to view a prompt before sending at some point an update was made so that prompts do not fill out your chat box when you press tab to complete one. we have prompts shared between our team and we can't expect people to run other's shared prompts without being able to read them. on top of that, many prompts are setup so that you finish it out yourself, and it's hard to do that if you can't read the prompt it would be ideal if it was an option. please add the ability to turn off anything you add, as this is a terrible feature for any reasonable developer. prompts are auto-loaded from mcps and extensions, this is dangerous really. cursor does this too, and it's not usable as a developer tool.
all_comments_text: yes makes sense, we want to allow you to click on the /prompt text, and it would open the prompt file. |SEP| fyi |SEP| to verify: - type a slash command that is backed by a file (e.g. [inline code] ) - move the mouse over it - verify that the mouse pointer turns into a 'open' mouse pointer - verify that you get a tooltip - verify that clicking opens the prompt file - verify that selections do not trigger the opening of the prompt file |SEP| this doesn't solve the issue for me at all. i don't use a mouse. everything else is keybindable and searchable with keyboard; models, agents, tools, etc. what is even the point of typing slash if you have to click anyways every time. is that really the user experience you want to provide? please give the option to always expand the prompt. your suggestion is needed either way though, so thanks for looking into that. |SEP| prompt files can also include attributes like tools and models that will be set, so expanding is not always possible. |SEP| prompt files can also include attributes like tools and models that will be set, so expanding is not always possible. i see your approach then, if you can't work out which prompt is in use after it's expanded. that's good to have too, turning on specific tools when needed for a prompt. thanks for explaining. |SEP| but i see your point. we need a way to open the prompt file with the keyboard. maybe using the command key on the link, like it is already in use in the editor for url links? |SEP| well let me throw in feature i have been wishing i had, which is the ability to load multiple prompts/skills in one chat. this is primarily for skills but i personally am using them identically to prompts 90% of the time. being able to put: [code block] would be great for building more hands-on workflows with copilot. with that in mind, my suggestion would be an "expand prompts" command, which would work on one prompt too, but you know the whole up setup best. |SEP| [inline code] is not really a prompt command, but additional context. we current only support one slash command per request [inline code] looks more like context [inline code] would be the way i think about this. but lets discuss this in another issue. |SEP| yeah they would skills which provide context usually, which are very closely related to prompts. this approach with [inline code] doesn't work with global context files, there's no way to tag these. which is essentially what i am suggesting. i would route my context via skills/prompts in this approach. i will create another issue about this once i have a concrete idea. interesting that you think of prompts as being separate from providing context, makes sense with the name [inline code] . i am probably misusing a bit with the lack of global context tagging. thanks again for the discussion :)

all_comments_text: hi , apologies for the delay, and thank you for bringing this to our attention. the root cause of this issue is a version mismatch between tensorflow and numpy. when tensorflow 2.14.0 was released, it was compiled against the numpy 1.x c api. starting with numpy 2.x, there were significant internal abi (application binary interface) changes. as a result, tensorflow versions compiled with numpy 1.x cannot be used with numpy 2.x leading to the following error: [code block] i have tested your use case with tensorflow 2.20.0 and numpy 2.3.4, and can confirm it works correctly. please refer to the attached screenshot for reference. you have two options: upgrade tensorflow to version 2.20.0 or newer [code block] if you must stay on tensorflow 2.14, downgrade numpy: [inline code] in general, it is best to use the latest stable tensorflow release to ensure compatibility with modern dependencies. thank you! |SEP| hi , thank you for your answer. it perfectly fix my error. but i have just a question: "why this resolution combination is valid? is it a bug from [inline code] or where it comes from? (i should not be able to install [inline code] with [inline code] )" thank you. |SEP| tf has c++ bits and compiles against numpy's c bits too. this means that they need to be compatible. tf 2.14 is too old. please switch to a newer tf if you want to use [inline code] also, note that the only versions supported in tf are those released in the past year, rolling (that is, 3-4 versions back). so, tf 2.14 is already outside of the support window.

specific to the presence of the [inline code] operator in the graph. a forward-only [inline code] graph compiles with a truly dynamic batch dimension as expected. background: i am working on a machine learning force field project and aim to accelerate my model's training process using torch.compile. briefly, the machine learning force field model reads distance vectors between atoms and outputs energy and forces, where the latter are derived by computing the gradient of energy with respect to the distance vectors. in other words, the forward() function of my model contains manually invoked autograd operations. additionally, the loss function of my model consists of two components (energy and forces), which means that higher-order gradients need to be computed during the backward pass of training. meanwhile, the "batch size" varies for each batch (since the number of atoms in each molecule is different), which requires that the code generated by compilation does not statically fix the dimensions of the input tensors. to address this, i first traced my computation graph using make fx(mode="symbolic"), then compiled the model with torch.compile(dynamic=true). however, i found that the compiled output still has statically fixed dimensions, leading to frequent recompilation when feeding inputs of different shapes. after analysis, i identified that the root cause of the clean_text: tensorflow nearestneighborsop crash due to negative k value issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? yes source source tensorflow version tf2.18 custom code yes os platform and distribution ubuntu 22.04 mobile device no response python version 3.10 bazel version no response gcc/compiler version no response cuda/cudnn version 12.4 gpu model and memory no response current behavior? tensorflow nearestneighborsop crash due to negative k value aborted (core dumped) standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]
all_comments_text: """ fix: invalid k parameter in nearestneighbors operation issue: k=-1 caused invalid argument error with message "expected shape dimensions to be non-negative, got - [hash] " root cause: - k parameter represents number of nearest neighbors to find - negative values are mathematically invalid for this operation - tensorflow's dimension validation rejects negative shape dimensions solution: - changed k from -1 to 1 (find single nearest neighbor) - k must be positive integer where 0 < k < = number of centers """ k = tf.constant(1, dtype=tf.int64) |SEP| hi , apologies for the delay, and thank you for raising this concern. the crash occurs because a negative value was passed for k in the nearestneighbors op: [inline code] the parameter k must be a positive integer (≥ 1), representing the number of nearest centers to return for each point. passing a negative value leads to an invalid tensor shape internally, which causes the runtime abort. i tried running the code with a valid k value, and it worked fine. here i am attaching a [gist]( [url] for your reference. thank you! |SEP| hi thank you so much for taking the time to analyze and fix this issue — really appreciate it! this was actually my first tensorflow bug report, and i’m really glad it helped improve the library. it would mean a lot if i could be mentioned or tagged as the reporter/contributor for this issue. thanks again for the detailed response and learning opportunity!
solution_comments: """ fix: invalid k parameter in nearestneighbors operation issue: k=-1 caused invalid argument error with message "expected shape dimensions to be non-negative, got - [hash] " root cause: - k parameter represents number of nearest neighbors to find - negative values are mathematically invalid for this operation - tensorflow's dimension validation rejects negative shape dimensions solution: - changed k from -1 to 1 (find single nearest neighbor) - k must be positive integer where 0 < k < = number of centers """ k = tf.constant(1, dtype=tf.int64) |SEP| hi thank you so much for taking the time to analyze and fix this issue — really appreciate it! this was actually my first tensorflow bug report, and i’m really glad it helped improve the library. it would mean a lot if i could be mentioned or tagged as the reporter/contributor for this issue. thanks again for the detailed response and learning opportunity!

clean_title: copilot chat agent mode: toolcallingloop stop hook intermittently terminates conversations with no response
clean_body: description when using copilot chat in agent mode with claude agent sdk (claude-opus-4.6), multi-turn conversations are intermittently terminated by the toolcallingloop stop hook returning shouldcontinue=false with reasons=undefined. this causes the user to see either "sorry, no response was returned" or the conversation silently stops — even when the claude api has successfully returned a valid response. environment - vs code: 1.111.0 - copilot chat extension: 0.39.0 / 0.39.1 (both affected) - connection: vs code remote ssh (vscode-server) - model: claude-opus-4.6 (via claude copilot proxy) - copilot sku: copilot for business seat quota root cause the toolcallingloop stop hook intermittently returns shouldcontinue=false with no reason, prematurely killing the agent loop: [info] ccreq:0496329b | success | claude-opus-4.6 - claude-opus-4-6 | 2779ms | [panel/editagent] [info] [toolcallingloop] stop hook result: shouldcontinue=false, reasons=undefined claude returned a valid response, but the stop hook discarded it. the user sees "sorry, no response was returned." behavior details - intermittent: the stop hook may fire on the first message, then work fine on retry. in one session, it fired twice in a row, then worked normally — same extension host, same version. - not version-specific: observed on both 0.39.0 and 0.39.1. downgrading does not help. - agent mode only: the issue only occurs in agent mode ([panel/editagent]). when requests go through [claude copilot proxy] directly (non-agent mode), they complete successfully with no stop hook interference. - first observed: 2026-03-12. not present in 2026-03-11 logs. additional errors in logs 1. error during execution — a downstream consequence of the stop hook issue. thrown in wo.handleresultmessage (extension.js:2128:11800) after the loop is prematurely terminated. observed 9 times in a single session, triggered by rate limiting (429), vision api errors (400), and even after successful api responses. 2. pendingmigrationerror: navigator is now a global in nodejs — occurs during extension initialization on every startup. may contribute to instability. 3. operation aborted — multiple instances in remoteexthost.log, thrown from x$e.write in the extension. expected behavior 1. the stop hook should not return shouldcontinue=false when claude has returned a valid response. 2. if the stop hook decides to terminate, reasons should contain an actionable explanation, not undefined. 3. transient api errors (429 rate limiting, 400 vision not enabled) should be handled gracefully — retry or skip, not terminate the entire conversation. workaround resend the same message. it sometimes succeeds on retry. however, retry suddenly never succeeded again.

clean_body: issue type bug have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? yes source source tensorflow version tf 2.21.0 custom code yes os platform and distribution no response mobile device no response python version no response bazel version no response gcc/compiler version no response cuda/cudnn version no response gpu model and memory no response current behavior? when using [inline code] to enable xla jit compilation, any function that receives string tensors as input fails to compile. the error occurs because xla's argument handling mechanism ( [inline code] operation) does not support string data types ( [inline code] ). the code works correctly without [inline code] , but fails when xla compilation is enabled. standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]

all_comments_text: i was able to reproduce the same issue using tensorflow versions 2.19.0 and 2.20.0. i am providing a [gist]( [url] here for your reference. thank you! |SEP| hi , this is intended behavior for xla. the xla compiler is optimized for numerical computations (linear algebra) and does not natively support [inline code] types or operations like [inline code] that rely on hash tables and variable-length data. when you use [inline code] , you force the entire function context to run on the xla device. since the function input is a string tensor, xla attempts to create an entry point ( [inline code] ) for a string, which fails because xla kernels only support numerical types (floats, ints, bools). solution: you must separate the preprocessing (strings - ints) from the computation (ints - output). the preprocessing should run in standard tensorflow (cpu), and the resulting integers can then be passed to the jit-compiled function. here is the corrected architectural pattern: python import tensorflow as tf from tensorflow import keras from tensorflow.keras import layers class testmodel(keras.model): def init (self): super(). init () 1. preprocessing (cpu/standard runtime) self.number encoding = layers.stringlookup(vocabulary=["1", "2", "3", "4", "5"]) 2. computation (xla compatible) self.embedding = layers.embedding(input dim=self.number encoding.vocabulary size(), output dim=64) def call(self, inputs): step a: convert strings to ints (not compiled) encoded = self.number encoding(inputs) step b: run math on xla (compiled) return self.compiled embedding(encoded) .function(jit compile=true) def compiled embedding(self, encoded inputs): this function only sees integers, so xla is good return self.embedding(encoded inputs) inputs = tf.constant(["3", "1", "4", "2", "5"]) model = testmodel() output = model(inputs) print("success:", output.shape) |SEP| hi , thanks for your patience. this is expected behavior. xla only supports numerical tensor types, so [inline code] inputs are not compatible when [inline code] is enabled. all inputs to a jit-compiled function need to be xla-supported types. the usual pattern is to keep preprocessing outside the jit-compiled function. after moving stringlookup out of the compiled function, the example runs correctly under xla. i have shared a minimal working [gist]( [url] for reference. -codes thanks for the helpful explanation.

clean_title: allow updating a system/admin vs code install from within vs code
clean_body: type: feature request tl;dr on windows systems where uac is disabled (enablelua = 0), all processes run elevated. in this configuration: the vs code user installer cannot run because it detects elevation. existing system installer installations cannot update automatically. this leaves manual reinstallation of the system installer as the only way to update vs code. it would be helpful if vs code supported updating system installations via an elevation prompt, allowing users in uac-disabled environments to update from within vs code rather than downloading the installer manually. ---------------------------------------------------------------------- environment windows 11 domain-joined machine vs code originally installed using the system installer uac disabled (enablelua = 0) user account is a domain user, not the built-in administrator problem when attempting to update vs code to the latest version, the built-in update mechanism does not work because the installation was performed using the system installer. the obvious alternative is to download and run the user installer, which normally allows updates without administrator privileges. however, when uac is disabled (enablelua = 0), all processes run with elevated privileges. as a result: the user installer refuses to run, displaying: “this user installer is not meant to be run as an administrator.” because uac is disabled, it is not possible to launch a non-elevated process. this means: the user installer cannot run the system installer cannot update automatically the only option is to manually download and reinstall the system installer each time observed behaviour existing vs code install uses the system installer auto-update does not apply because elevation is required attempting to run the user installer fails because the process is detected as elevated disabling elevation is impossible when enablelua = 0 result users in environments where uac is disabled cannot use the vs code user installer and also cannot update a system installer installation from within vs code. updates therefore require manually downloading and reinstalling vs code each time. suggested improvement possible improvements that would resolve this scenario: allow the user installer to run when elevated if the target install is in the user profile. allow vs code to download and apply updates to a system install by prompting for elevation. provide an “update with elevation” option inside vs code when the installation type requires it. why this matters in corporate or domain environments where uac is disabled or restricted by policy, the current behaviour makes updating vs code unnecessarily difficult. allowing an elevation prompt for updates (similar to many other windows applications) would make updates straightforward regardless of installation type. vs code version: code 1.106.3 ( [hash] , 2025-11-25t22:28:18.024z) os version: windows nt x64 10.0.26100 modes:

all_comments_text: the issue is incompatibility with cuda compute 12: one workaround which worked for me is use ngc tf containers which extends support for blackwell architecture: [code block] run and chill |SEP| , thank you for the quick and helpful response to the user. hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for raising this issue. currently, tensorflow official releases are tested and built up to cuda 12.5. since cuda 12.8 and blackwell (compute capability 12.0a) are newer, tensorflow wheels currently fall back to jit-compiling ptx kernels, which can result in longer initialization times. as mentioned, using the nvidia ngc tensorflow container is a valid temporary workaround those builds include newer cuda/cudnn versions that extend support for blackwell gpus. you can refer to the official [tensorflow compatibility documentation]( [url] here for the latest supported cuda and cudnn versions. thank you! |SEP| in my case, using a docker container was not desired, so i attempted to repackage the already built package and use it directly on the host (linux x86 64, python 3.12, nvidia rtx pro 4000 blackwell). aside from the following ignorable warning, everything worked as expected: [code block] [code block]
solution_comments: the issue is incompatibility with cuda compute 12: one workaround which worked for me is use ngc tf containers which extends support for blackwell architecture: [code block] run and chill |SEP| , thank you for the quick and helpful response to the user. hi , apologies for the delay, and thanks for raising this issue. currently, tensorflow official releases are tested and built up to cuda 12.5. since cuda 12.8 and blackwell (compute capability 12.0a) are newer, tensorflow wheels currently fall back to jit-compiling ptx kernels, which can result in longer initialization times. as mentioned, using the nvidia ngc tensorflow container is a valid temporary workaround those builds include newer cuda/cudnn versions that extend support for blackwell gpus. you can refer to the official [tensorflow compatibility documentation]( [url] here for the latest supported cuda and cudnn versions. thank you!

clean_title: vs code becomes unresponsive while github copilot chat generates multi-file patches
clean_body: type: bug type: bug when using github copilot chat in vs code to perform multi-file refactoring/editing, the entire vs code window becomes heavily unresponsive while copilot is generating or applying patches. this is not limited to the edited file. the whole ide is affected: - editor becomes sluggish or freezes - terminal responsiveness is degraded - sidebar/tree view becomes slow - general ui interaction is delayed while copilot is processing changes steps to reproduce 1. open a project in vs code 2. open github copilot chat 3. ask copilot to refactor or update multiple files/components 4. let copilot start generating patches / applying edits across files 5. while it is in states such as "generating patch", "processing", or similar, try interacting with: - editor - terminal - explorer/sidebar - other open files actual result the whole vs code window becomes unresponsive or very slow during copilot's patch generation/editing workflow. expected result copilot processing should not degrade the responsiveness of the entire ide window. even if patch generation is heavy, it should be isolated enough that the editor, terminal, and general ui remain usable. additional context this does not appear to be a general vs code, project, or hardware limitation in my case. i can reproduce this in the same project , on the same pc , in the same vs code environment , with no other heavy workloads running . for comparison, when using the openai codex extension locally in this same project and on this same machine, this ide-wide unresponsiveness does not happen. code can be generated and files can be edited without causing the whole vs code window to become sluggish or freeze. this strongly suggests the problem is specific to github copilot chat's patch generation/editing workflow, rather than a general issue with the project itself or my machine. the problem is especially noticeable during larger multi-file edits/refactors. notes this feels like copilot's background processing is impacting the responsiveness of the full vs code window too aggressively. even if the feature is computationally expensive, it should not make the whole ide stall just because it is editing files. please let me know if logs, performance traces, or a minimal repro workflow would help. extension version: 0.39.0 vs code version: code 1.111.0 ( [hash] , all_comments_text: additional data point: i reproduced this again on the same machine and same project with no other significant workload running. observed during the slowdown: - vs code process group around 1.4 gb memory - dart.exe around 800 mb memory - system cpu was not saturated - total memory usage around 73% most importantly, in this same project and on this same pc, using the openai codex extension locally does not cause this ide-wide unresponsiveness. this makes it look much more like a copilot chat-specific performance bug/regression than a general vs code or hardware limitation. |SEP| 这是 copilot chat 特定的后台处理影响主线程响应的问题。 建议的解决方案： 1. 进程隔离 ：将 patch 生成/应用工作移到 web worker 或独立进程中，这样繁重的计算不会阻塞主 ui 线程 2. 增量处理 ：将多文件编辑拆分为增量任务，使用 requestidlecallback 或分批处理，避免一次性占用太多资源 3. 内存管理 ：对于大型项目，考虑实现虚拟化或按需加载，减少内存占用 4. 优先级队列 ：实现任务优先级，确保用户交互（如输入、点击）始终有最高优先级 桌面应用（如 openclaw）通常使用独立的 worker 进程来处理 ai 任务，通过消息传递与主 ui 通信，这样可以确保即使 ai 处理繁重，ui 仍然保持流畅。这种架构值得参考。 希望这些建议有帮助！ |SEP| related: [url] |SEP| please grab a profile: [url]

all_comments_text: cc |SEP| i am working on it. would try to come up with a fix soon |SEP| i have raised the pr for this issue. ptal |SEP| we have [inline code] to skip dynamic shapes in cudagraph regions. we could similarly add a skip for unbacked shapes only. but i'm not sure the current behavior is a bug ? there's nothing unsafe about taking in an unbacked int. cc |SEP| i agree. but if this unbacked int is data dependent, we need a way to make any operations related to it cudagraph unsafe.. in the vllm use-case specifically, we don't want to skip all dynamic sizes as we mark the batch size as dynamic and then we'd be skipping the whole graph. we might also transition the batch size to unbacked in the future so if we do that we need a way to distinguish between the batch size (cudagraph safe) and the split point (cudagraph unsafe). the most general solution would be to have a way of "tagging" unbacked and dynamic ints as cudagraph safe/unsafe. that way users can directly decide which dims are cudagraph safe and which aren't. |SEP| yes, agreed we should add an api for tagging particular symints. in this instance, it's perhaps unprofitable, but it's not unsafe, since we add guards/respecialize for each unique input. |SEP| this might be due to vllm dropping guards but where does the specialization happen? because we did not see it happen and so the answer was wrong because the data-dependent shapes got locked into cudagraphs. hoping to discuss adding this api at the thursday sync. |SEP| there is a mismatch between pt cudagraph tree and vllm cudagraphwrapper the graph partition signature is [code block] pt2 cudagraph tree collects all ints in the arguments ( [inline code] in this example) and find the corresponding cudagraph. [url] in vllm, we wrap cudagraphwrapper on [inline code] . it does not check all ints in the arguments (thus ignoring [inline code] ). it assumes the only dynamic shape is batch size and only checks the [inline code] (aka batch size). [url] going forward, we can mark an unbacked symint as cg unsafe and exclude all related ops. |SEP| 2.11 milestone for discussion |SEP| cc any thoughts on an api that marks specific [inline code] s as cudagraph-unsafe? |SEP| if we are good with adding the ability to mark specific symints as cudgraph-unsafe, has bandwidth to implement the feature. |SEP| api that marks specific [inline code] s as cudagraph-unsafe? the idea is fine for me,but, can we use terminology other than safe / unsafe for dynamic shapes that are allowed or not allowed in cuda graphs? it seems like everything is “safe” in the sense of correctness, but inductor will perform dynamic dispatch on dynamic shapes and recapture the graph. what we want is to control when to allow dynamic shapes in a cuda graph. maybe something like cudagraph capturable? for example: torch. dynamo.set cudagraph capturable(s0/u0, true/false) also, just for clarification: when s0 is not cudagraph capturable, we will skip the entire graph containing it if graph partition=false. if graph partition=true, we will split the graph—i.e., find the maximal subgraph that does not include s0 and cuda-graph that part this is something that happens automatically in inductor, right, and not something vllm has to handle manually? is my understanding correct? |SEP| torch. dynamo.set cudagraph capturable(s0/u0, true/false)? my concern is that users may not know which s0/u0 to mark. it might be fine for s0 as [inline code] . but it's a bit hard to know exactly the name as u0 (or some other number like u1728). maybe it's easier to mark a custom op such that all of its output symints are cudagraph unsafe? when s0 is not cudagraph capturable, we will not skip the whole graph that have it if graph partition=false, if graph partition=true we will wsplit it, namely find the maximal subgraph that does not include s0 and cuda-graph that part. iiuc this config should only have impact on inductor graph partition. inductor graph partition identifies all ops that use these cg-unsafe symints and splits these ops outside graph partitions. -turnansky thanks for help! i have a pr to potentially fix the issue since there are some api design. |SEP| we also need to think when this annotations happens, the easiest idea would be to mark the symint as cudagraph safe/unsafe upon creation (or included/excluded - sorry laith). but i guess then we're mixing concerns a little bit |SEP| i feel the confusion here mostly stems around the fact that we occasionally do support dynamic shapes with cudagraphs (batch size, by retracing the cudagraph for multiple sizes). in principle, if you had a cuda graph unsafe op that returned a tensor of size u0, the extent processing this u0 could be cudagraphed if you were willing to record cudagraphs for multiple distinct values of u0. is this actually a good idea? well with multiple symbols you will quickly get an explosion of possible combos, so maybe not. if you only care about identifying the batch dynamic symbol, life is relatively simple: there is one symbol which is ok to cudagraph and all the other symbols are not ok. the api here ought to be related to whatever is also setting up the dispatch/record multiple graphs per size. |SEP| if the batch size is the only “cuda graph–safe” dynamic dimension, then we can add an argument to mark dynamic and mark unbacked as well, and disallow everything else. are there any other dynamic shapes we want to allow in cuda graphs for the vllm use case? |SEP| are there any other dynamic shapes we want to allow in cuda graphs for the vllm use case? not that i know of, because we currently have a linear amount of cudagraphs and with any other size we'd get a quadratic amount which is unsustainable. the only other case would be if a symint is constrained to the batch size but hopefully then we can express it in terms of batch size directly. for example, if we want to pad the batch size to a multiple of 4 for specific ops, we'd want to make sure that works correctly and doesn't get ops excluded from cudagraphs. |SEP| are there any other dynamic shapes we want to allow in cuda graphs for the vllm use case? not that i know of, because we currently have a linear amount of cudagraphs and with any other size we'd get a quadratic amount which is unsustainable. the only other case would be if a symint is constrained to the batch size but hopefully then we can express it in terms of batch size directly. for example, if we want to pad the batch size to a multiple of 4 for specific ops, we'd want to make sure that works correctly and doesn't get ops excluded from cudagraphs. if we did padding the size will be a function of the batch size, i dont think that would be an issue.

all_comments_text: just curious, how did you get to this reproducer? but this looks like a good example of bad api design, as this aten op should never have been a public function |SEP| this issue has already been fixed in the main branch via pr (merged january 13, 2026). the fix added a device check for [inline code] tensor in all packed sequence rnn operations (lstm, gru, rnn relu, rnn tanh) for both cudnn and miopen backends. with the fix , you'll get a clear error message instead of a segfault: [code block] the fix will be available in the next pytorch release after 2.10.0. in the meantime, you can: 1. use a nightly build: [inline code] 2. keep [inline code] on cpu (which is the expected behavior) cc |SEP| we're taking a look at test input generation for aten kernels, which is why i wound up testing this kernel specifically. |SEP| thank you! i installed the nightly build and validated the fix.
solution_comments: this issue has already been fixed in the main branch via pr (merged january 13, 2026). the fix added a device check for [inline code] tensor in all packed sequence rnn operations (lstm, gru, rnn relu, rnn tanh) for both cudnn and miopen backends. with the fix , you'll get a clear error message instead of a segfault: [code block] the fix will be available in the next pytorch release after 2.10.0. in the meantime, you can: 1. use a nightly build: [inline code] 2. keep [inline code] on cpu (which is the expected behavior) cc |SEP| thank you! i installed the nightly build and validated the fix.

clean_title: [regression] torch.compile does not recompile when class attribute is mutated between calls
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug torch.compile returns incorrect results when a class attribute used inside the compiled function is mutated between calls. the compiled graph seems to reuse the value captured at the first trace, even though the attribute has since changed. this is a correctness regression: the same test passes on torch 2.2 but fails on 2.10. [code block] error information: [code block] versions [code block] cc -chen -nrv

clean_title: bug? integrated terminal font rendering appears thin / poorly antialiased in light themes on macos
clean_body: does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: yes/no type: bug platform: macos vs code version: 1.111.0 (observed in recent updates) environment: occurs across multiple macbook devices with different screen resolutions description in recent versions of vs code, the integrated terminal appears to have a font rendering issue when using light themes on macos . when both the editor theme and terminal theme are light , terminal fonts appear overly thin with visible pixel zig-zag artifacts , suggesting that font antialiasing is not being applied correctly. this significantly reduces readability. this behavior does not occur when: - using dark editor themes - using a light editor theme with a dark terminal theme the issue appears to affect all light themes tested , not just a specific theme. observed effects - terminal text appears very thin and jagged - antialiasing artifacts are visible - powerline / nerd fonts icons become difficult to distinguish - tui box-drawing characters become hard to read examples: - the frame around the “openai codex” banner in the screenshot becomes almost illegible. - similar rendering artifacts appear in other terminal-based tools that rely on text ui elements. ![image]( [url] ![image]( [url] steps to reproduce 1. use macos 2. install the latest version of vs code 3. select a light editor theme 4. use a light terminal theme 5. open the integrated terminal 6. use a terminal font such as nerd fonts / powerline fonts 7. run a tui application (example: codex cli) expected behavior terminal fonts should render with proper antialiasing and normal stroke thickness , consistent with how they appear: - in dark vs code themes - in native macos terminal or iterm - in vs code when using dark terminal themes troubleshooting attempted the following were tested but did not resolve the issue : - disabling macos system font antialiasing - enabling or disabling terminal font weight settings - setting the following in [inline code] : [code block] - testing across multiple macbook devices - testing across different display resolutions - testing with multiple light themes [code block]

clean_title: [dtensor]inconsistent random number generation in torch.distributed.tensor.randn
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug bug description: when using torch.distributed.tensor.randn to create dtensors with fixed random seeds, the generated tensors are inconsistent between single-gpu and multi-gpu scenarios. specifically, creating a (4, 4) tensor on a single gpu produces different results compared to creating (2, 4) tensors on two gpus separately, even when using the same random seed. expected behavior: with the same random seed, the distributed tensor creation should maintain consistency with single-device semantics. the concatenation of multi-gpu results should match the single-gpu result when using equivalent global shapes and seeds. actual behavior: the random values generated in distributed mode differ from single-device mode, breaking reproducibility and debugging capabilities. versions torch 2.9.1 cuda 12.4 python scripy [inline code] cc -huang -l

all_comments_text: from the linker documentation the –b symbolic(functions) options are intended for specialized dynamic objects and are not recommended for general use. although these options can reduce runtime relocation overhead, they can compromise bindings that expect interposition. to reduce runtime relocation processing, the creation of a version definition or use of direct bindings is recommended over using these options. in other words it is an optimization technique to shorten relocation times. framework.so is by design supposed to be imported by many other libraries (all tf's own python bindings and many other libs which depend on it). yes, it does not work well, but that is a different discussion, and a decision which had been made many years ago, so we are stuck with it. main point of this issue is the need to control and limit exported symbols by providing version script to framework.so.2, though matching pr ( [url] introduces version script which still exports [everything ]( [url] yes, the general idea of properly controlling exported symbols is what we would like to have, the bigger [inline code] has it (but still exports tons of stuff), [inline code] by design is a small subset of tf, that can be exported by anyone, thus its entire body is exported. in other words, the version script part did not change the behavior, the symbolic-functions arg broke mac build, and i don't think the symbolic optimization technique is appropriate for a binary which is being loaded all over the place (both automatically via dynamic section resolution and often all_comments_text: i was able to reproduce this issue locally using the provided example. the root cause seems to be the use of tf.variable for a static configuration value (d model). when compiling with xla (jit compile=true), shape and configuration values need to be python primitives. replacing self.d model = tf.variable(d model) with self.d model = d model resolves the error completely on my end. i’ve opened a small documentation pr to clarify this usage so others don’t run into the same issue. |SEP| i was able to reproduce the same issue using tensorflow 2.21.0-rc0 as well as the nightly version. please find the [gist]( [url] for your reference. thank you! |SEP| hi -tree thank you for your answer , yes, replacing [inline code] with [inline code] , will works fine.when xla tries to parse the variable as a static shape, it raises the error. i have attached a gist for your [reference]( [url]

all_comments_text: hi , could you please provide the tensorflow version and the compatible packages you are trying to install? there are at least a few possible causes for the issue you are facing: the msvc 2019 redistributable is not installed. your cpu does not support avx2 instructions. your cpu or python installation is 32-bit. a required library is missing or located in a different path and cannot be loaded. additionally, please share your environment details and the steps you followed to install tensorflow. also, it appears this issue might be a duplicate of [url] [url] thank you! |SEP| i have intel celeron n4120 processor with the memory 4gb ram and i am student to develop many project yesterday i create a project so i installed tensorflow using pip command : pip install opencv-python mediapipe numpy and pip install tensorflow==2.18.0 |SEP| i reinstalled tensorflow i sent photo![20250930 203917.jpg]( [url] |SEP| hi , based on the error and your system specs (intel celeron n4120 with 4gb ram), i can help identify the likely cause: root cause: the intel celeron n4120 processor does not support avx2 instructions, which tensorflow 2.18 requires. this is why you're getting the dll initialization error. solutions: 1. use tensorflow 2.10 or earlier (last version with avx-only support): [code block] 2. install msvc 2019 redistributable (if not already installed): - download from: [url] - this provides required dll dependencies 3. verify python is 64-bit : [code block] should output [inline code] . if it shows [inline code] , reinstall 64-bit python. 4. alternative: use tensorflow-cpu with conda (if other methods fail): [code block] for your gesture recognition project with limited resources, i'd recommend tensorflow 2.10.1 + optimizing your model for inference. let me know if you need help with model optimization! clean_title: render some distinction with default approvals when using copilot cli worktree
clean_body: - select copilot cli as agent type, worktree as isolation mode, and default approvals - ask the copilot cli to edit package.json - ❓ copilot cli makes the edit without asking for permission, since it's working on a worktree - if i try the same flow with workspace instead of worktree, or with the local agent instead of copilot cli, then i get prompted as expected with default approvals selected should we provide some sort of indicator to users somewhere about this behavior? right now, the explanation for approvals mentions using my configured settings - which settings? and are these settings different for different agent harnesses?

all_comments_text: raised a pr, its working but not sure if this is the best way to do it |SEP| if we have to have a solution from fsdp2 side, the solution is [inline code] . it will keep transformer parameters unsharded during backward. if we own cache implementation, cache should be able to do this: torch.tensor - fsdpmodule - fsdpmodule.unshard() before backward - fsdpmodule.reshard() after backward see my script to use [inline code] to workaround [code block] |SEP| closing my pr, reshard after forward=false fixes the issue on the original repro code |SEP| hey folks — i’d ask you to take another look at this, as it seems to be broader than just independent / unused outputs. consider a model distillation setup (e.g., [dmd]( [url] for diffusion models) where we have a very large teacher model wrapped with fsdp using [inline code] . the model cannot be materialized on a single gpu. during the “fake” pass (when computing both scores), the teacher is run under [inline code] . with [inline code] , the model remains sharded after each layer forward; otherwise, it gets gathered and immediately ooms. the issue is that running under [inline code] prevents the teacher from participating in the loss computation, which then results in an error. however, reshard after forward=true is required here to avoid the gather/oom. rewrapping or restructuring the teacher model is not a practical option, as we don’t own its implementation — it can be a model from e.g. [inline code] . please note that this behavior was not an issue in [inline code] . to reproduce the pattern in a minimal setup, you can replace [inline code] with: [code block] |SEP| thanks for sharing a real use-case for this i can spend some time in the dmd example and come up with a recipe. .unshard / .reshard api is probably enough. please note that this behavior was not an issue in [inline code] . is there a repro? this is all about calling .unshard / .reshard in forward/ backward hooks. to reproduce the pattern in a minimal setup, you can replace [inline code] with: [code block] what is the cached intermediate like? solution_comments: a small workaround for people facing this issue 1. click on try downloading manually in the error pop up 2. it downloads a extension name.vsixpackage file and the downloaded file is not visible in the install from vsix file selector 3. rename extension name.vsixpackage - extension name.vsix 4. now try using the install from vsix option in the extensions it shows the renamed file 5. select the renamed file and the extension downloads and installs normally

clean_title: padding for nested long tensor loses precision
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug the padding for nested long tensor ends to loss of precision. [code block] as can be seen, the padding value loses precision. note that rest of the values don't lose precision (e.g. [inline code] in the sample don't lose precision but in the padding loses the precision) versions [code block] cc
clean_text: padding for nested long tensor loses precision 🐛 describe the bug the padding for nested long tensor ends to loss of precision. [code block] as can be seen, the padding value loses precision. note that rest of the values don't lose precision (e.g. [inline code] in the sample don't lose precision but in the padding loses the precision) versions [code block] cc
all_comments_text: the precision loss occurs because the padding parameter is strictly typed as float in the aten dispatcher definition (mapping to c++ double), leading to irreversible mantissa truncation for 64-bit integers exceeding 53 bits before being passed to the tensor fill operation. [code block] [code block] |SEP| i was able to reproduce this on google colab (python 3.12, pytorch 2.10.0+cpu). reproduction script: pythonimport torch from torch import tensor from torch.nested import nested tensor sample = nested tensor([ tensor([1, - [hash] ]), tensor([- [hash] ]) ]) padded = torch.nested.to padded tensor(sample, padding=- [hash] ) print(padded) expected = - [hash] actual = padded[1][1].item() print(f"expected: {expected}") print(f"got: {actual}") print(f"precision lost: {expected != actual}") output: tensor([[ 1, - [hash] ], [- [hash] , - [hash] ]]) expected: - [hash] got: - [hash] precision lost: true this confirms the bug. the discrepancy of 112 is consistent with the float64 mantissa truncation described by -logic — 64-bit integers beyond 53 bits of precision are silently corrupted when the padding value is cast through a c++ double. |SEP| can you help triage this? |SEP| as -logic mentions the number is not representable as cpp double, so not much we can do here

all_comments_text: hey! thanks for the solid repro code. it looks like xla is being a bit picky here. even though you’re using constants, the compiler is seeing them as tf.tensor objects rather than plain integers, and extract patches requires those dimensions to be static during the jit compilation. as a quick fix to get your model running, if you swap out the tf.constant math for regular python math—like sizes=[1, self.window size 2 + 1, 1, 1]—it should compile fine since window size is already known. i'm going to dig into the source to see if we can make the compiler smarter about folding those constants automatically so it matches eager mode behavior! |SEP| this should be fixed now via

clean_title: read file image support (pr ) broken when model receives v1 tool schema (startline/endline)
clean_body: bug the image reading support added in [microsoft/vscode-copilot-chat]( [url] doesn't work when the model receives the v1 [inline code] tool schema (which has required [inline code] / [inline code] parameters). repro 1. open vs code insiders with copilot chat pre-release (v0.40. [hash] , which includes the merged pr) 2. in agent mode, ask the model to read an image file by path 3. the model calls [inline code] with the image path — but it must include [inline code] and [inline code] because those are required in its tool schema 4. the image handler rejects the call: [code block] root cause the pr's image detection code always rejects v1 params: [code block] - v2 schema uses [inline code] / [inline code] (optional) → model can omit them → image reading works ✅ - v1 schema uses [inline code] / [inline code] (required) → model must always provide them → [inline code] is hardcoded to [inline code] → image reading always fails ❌ some models (e.g. claude) still receive the v1 tool definition with required [inline code] / [inline code] , making image reading completely inaccessible. expected behavior [inline code] should be able to read image files regardless of which tool schema version the model receives. suggested fix either: 1. in the v1 path, ignore [inline code] / [inline code] when the file is an image (don't treat v1 as always having line params), or 2. ensure all models receive the v2 schema where [inline code] / [inline code] are optional environment - vs code insiders (march 13, 2026) - copilot chat pre-release v0.40. [hash] - model: claude (receives v1 read file schema) cc

clean_title: importerror: dll load failed for pywrap tensorflow internal when using tensorflow via jep in java app
clean_body: issue type support have you reproduced the bug with tensorflow nightly? no source source tensorflow version tf 2.19 and tf 2.20 custom code yes os platform and distribution windows 11 version 25h2 mobile device no response python version python 3.12 and 3.13 bazel version no response gcc/compiler version no response cuda/cudnn version not installed gpu model and memory no response current behavior? hi, i am running some python code from my java app through jep. i am not in the process of upgrading python and related packages, and i encountered the following issue: [code block] i know there are many similar issues reported online, but this seems very specific to the tensorflow version i am using. observed behavior: - python 3.12 + tensorflow 2.18 + tensorboard 2.18 + tensorflow-intel 2.18 + tensorflow-estimator 2.15.0 + jep 4.3.1 → works fine - python 3.12 + tensorflow 2.19 + tensorboard 2.19 + jep 4.3.1 → importerror occurs - python 3.13 + tensorflow 2.20 (and related packages) → importerror occurs interestingly, the error does not happen when i run the same python code from the command line, even with tensorflow 2.19 or 2.20. the issue only occurs when running via my java app through jep. everything used to work fine with older versions of tensorflow and python. things i have checked: 1. microsoft visual c++ redistributable → installed and up to date 2. cpu supports avx2 instructions → yes 3. python is 64-bit → yes 4. required dlls and libraries → same setup works with tensorflow 2.18 (and polder version), so paths and environment variables seem correct given this, i am wondering: did anything substantial change in tensorflow 2.19 that could cause this issue? could jep affect the order in which dlls are found and loaded, causing this problem with tensorflow 2.19+? any insight or suggestions would be greatly appreciated. thank you in advance for your help! standalone code to reproduce the issue [code block] relevant log output [code block]

all_comments_text: can you use [tf-java]( [url] for this? |SEP| can you use [tf-java]( [url] for this? thanks for the suggestion. we mainly use tensorflow through modules like tf.keras and tf.random, and some of the python code is client-provided, so we don’t have full control over it and they’re likely to rely on these training modules. from what we’ve seen, tensorflow java is more like a model loading/inference and doesn’t really support running or replacing keras-style training code, so it’s probably not a great fit for this use case. |SEP| in that case, this is a duplicate |SEP| tf-java can train models, but we haven't finished a keras style layer on top of the tf ops, so it's rather tricky to use at the moment. it's not going to support converting python code into java model code so it's probably a non-starter then.

clean_title: [mps] meta kernel for scaled dot product attention math for mps guards on dynamic seq len, breaking torch.export
clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug the meta kernel for [inline code] branches on [inline code] , which creates a data-dependent guard that prevents [inline code] from supporting dynamic sequence lengths. introduced in (eccc7be). minimal reproduction [code block] error: [code block] expected behavior export succeeds with [inline code] as a fully dynamic dimension covering [inline code] . root cause in [inline code] , [inline code] has: [code block] the three code paths ( [inline code] , [inline code] , [inline code] ) return different output shapes. during tracing, the branch on [inline code] specializes, creating guards that reject all other [inline code] values. proposed fix always return [inline code] shapes from the meta kernel — [inline code] for output, [inline code] for attention. the runtime kernel still dispatches to the optimal implementation; the meta kernel only needs correct shapes for tracing. this is safe for the first return value (the actual output) because the vector paths require [inline code] , so [inline code] is always correct. the second return value is never consumed — all c++ callsites use [inline code] . [inline code] compares both return values against the real kernel and will need updating for the 2pass case: the 2pass runtime path returns a 5d intermediate [inline code] vs the meta kernel's 4d [inline code] . since the second return value is universally discarded and already inconsistent across paths (general→4d attn weights, 2pass→5d intermediate, full attention→duplicate of output), the test should only compare the first return value. versions regression bisected to [hash] (). tested on [inline code] . cc

clean_body: 🐛 describe the bug when compiling aten.affine grid generator with torch.compile(..., backend="inductor") on cuda, the output shows large numerical discrepancies compared to eager mode when using float16 or bfloat16. the same code produces numerically consistent results in float32, suggesting this is a low-precision correctness issue specific to inductor. the error magnitude significantly exceeds typical floating-point noise and violates torch.testing.assert close with default tolerances. minimal reproduction [code block] run results [code block] versions [code block] cc -chen -nrv

all_comments_text: hi -tree , apologies for the delay, and thanks for reporting the issue. i was able to reproduce this on colab with tensorflow 2.20.0. once the code wrapped in [inline code] , tracing becomes stricter and the op signature is enforced more closely. in the case of [inline code] , the seed argument is expected to be a python integer, so passing a [inline code] causes a typeerror during tracing/xla compilation. when you run the same code in eager mode, tensorflow is more permissive, which is why this appears to work. that said, this is not guaranteed behavior and is not something the api officially supports. as a workaround, i tried switching to [inline code] , and this works as expected both in eager mode and with xla enabled. i have added colab [gist]( [url] for your reference with the updated code. let is know if this workaround works for you or if you are still seeing issues. thank you!

all_comments_text: hi , thanks for reaching out. we have a detailed guide on how to build tensorflow lite with cmake and use it in your own project. you can find it [here]( [url] specifically, the section "create a cmake project which uses tensorflow lite" should be very helpful for what you are trying to achieve. it even includes a cmakelists.txt example from our minimal example. we want to inform you that the core tensorflow lite project has formally transitioned and is now actively being developed as [litert]( [url] all future active development including bug fixes, performance improvements and feature enhancements for the runtime will occur exclusively within the new standalone litert repository. we strongly recommend migrating your current setup to litert to ensure you are utilizing the most up-to-date and supported version. finally, please note that all new bug reports and feature requests must be created directly in the [litert repository]( [url] going forward. if you choose to remain on the legacy tflite version instead of migrating, please share the specific reason(s) for your dependency as this feedback is crucial for understanding community-wide transition blockers. thanks for your cooperation and understanding.
